Our key insight is to employ the semantics-preserving transformations as the intermediate representation, which serves as both a generative mid-training task and the guidance for subsequent instruction-specific code transformations. Across general code editing and optimization tasks (e.g., GPU kernel optimization), SemRep outperforms the extensively finetuned baselines with strictly the same training budget by 6.9% in correctness, 1.1x in performance, 13.9% in generalization, and 6.7% in robustness. With the improved exploration of diverse code transformations, SemRep is particularly amenable to evolutionary search. Combined with an evolutionary coding agent, SemRep finds optimizations that 685B larger-weight baselines fail to discover while achieving the same performance with 25% less inference compute.

AI.tldr: SemRep improves code transformation by using semantics-preserving transformations as an intermediate representation that serves both as generative mid-training and as guidance for instruction-specific code transformations, outperforming existing methods across various metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches treat code transformation as end-to-end learning tasks, leaving representation construction implicit in model weights or relying on rigid compiler-level abstractions, which limits effectiveness. The need is for a high-quality code representation that better characterizes input code semantics to guide transformation.
AI.method: SemRep employs semantics-preserving transformations as intermediate representation, which serves dual purpose: as generative mid-training task and as guidance for subsequent instruction-specific code transformations. This framework is particularly amenable to evolutionary search when combined with evolutionary coding agents.
AI.result: Across general code editing and optimization tasks (including GPU kernel optimization), SemRep outperforms extensively finetuned baselines with same training budget: 6.9% improvement in correctness, 1.1x better performance, 13.9% better generalization, and 6.7% improvement in robustness. Combined with evolutionary search, it finds optimizations that 685B larger-weight baselines fail to discover while achieving same performance with 25% less inference compute.
AI.conclusion: SemRep demonstrates that using semantics-preserving transformations as generative intermediate representation significantly improves code transformation effectiveness across multiple dimensions, and shows particular promise when combined with evolutionary search techniques for discovering novel optimizations.

title: BrainBench: Exposing the Commonsense Reasoning Gap in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve impressive scores on standard benchmarks yet routinely fail questions that any human would answer correctly in seconds. We introduce BrainBench, a benchmark of 100 brainteaser questions spanning 20 carefully designed categories, each targeting a specific commonsense reasoning failure mode in LLMs. Categories range from implicit physical constraints ("Should I walk or drive my rental car to the return lot?") to semantic scope tricks and default assumption hijacks. We evaluate eight frontier models -- four from the Claude family and four from the GPT family -- using a zero-shot protocol with 10 independent runs per question. The best model, Claude Opus 4.6 with extended thinking, achieves only 80.3% accuracy; the worst, GPT-4o, scores 39.7%. Even top-performing models exhibit a 6-16 percentage-point gap between accuracy and consistency, revealing stochastic reasoning. Cross-lingual evaluation in Chinese shows most models degrade by 2-8 percentage points, confirming that these failures reflect reasoning deficits rather than language-specific artifacts. BrainBench provides a fine-grained diagnostic tool for identifying where and why LLMs substitute surface heuristics for genuine commonsense reasoning.

AI.tldr: BrainBench is a benchmark of 100 brainteaser questions across 20 categories designed to expose commonsense reasoning failures in LLMs, revealing that even top models perform poorly and show inconsistent reasoning.
AI.motivation: Large language models achieve high scores on standard benchmarks but fail simple questions that humans answer easily, indicating they lack genuine commonsense reasoning despite appearing intelligent.
AI.method: Created BrainBench with 100 brainteaser questions across 20 categories targeting specific commonsense reasoning failure modes. Evaluated 8 frontier models (4 Claude, 4 GPT) with zero-shot protocol and 10 independent runs per question. Also conducted cross-lingual evaluation in Chinese.
AI.result: Best model (Claude Opus 4.6 with extended thinking) achieved only 80.3% accuracy, worst (GPT-4o) scored 39.7%. Top models showed 6-16 percentage-point gap between accuracy and consistency, revealing stochastic reasoning. Chinese evaluation showed most models degraded by 2-8 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: BrainBench reveals LLMs rely on surface heuristics rather than genuine commonsense reasoning, and provides a fine-grained diagnostic tool for identifying specific reasoning failure modes in language models.

title: PLUME: Building a Network-Native Foundation Model for Wireless Traces via Protocol-Aware Tokenization
comment: 14-pages, 802.11 foundation model, matches frontier LLMs with 600x fewer params via protocol-aware tokenization, 5 figures, 12 tables, AUROC>=0.99 for zero-shot anomaly detection
summary: Foundation models succeed when they learn in the native structure of a modality, whether morphology-respecting tokens in language or pixels in vision. Wireless packet traces deserve the same treatment: meaning emerges from layered headers, typed fields, timing gaps, and cross-packet state machines, not flat strings. We present Plume (Protocol Language Understanding Model for Exchanges), a compact 140M-parameter foundation model for 802.11 traces that learns from structured PDML dissections. A protocol-aware tokenizer splits along the dissector field tree, emits gap tokens for timing, and normalizes identifiers, yielding 6.2x shorter sequences than BPE with higher per token information density. Trained on a curated corpus, Plume achieves 74-97% next-packet token accuracy across five real-world failure categories and AUROC >= 0.99 for zero-shot anomaly detection. On the same prediction task, frontier LLMs (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4) score comparably despite receiving identical protocol context, yet Plume does so with > 600x fewer parameters, fitting on a single GPU at effectively zero marginal cost vs. cloud API pricing, enabling on-prem, privacy-preserving root cause analysis.

AI.tldr: Plume is a 140M-parameter foundation model for 802.11 wireless packet traces that uses protocol-aware tokenization to learn from structured PDML dissections, achieving high accuracy with 600x fewer parameters than frontier LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current models treat wireless packet traces as flat strings, ignoring their inherent structure with layered headers, typed fields, timing gaps, and cross-packet state machines. This loses the semantic meaning that emerges from the native structure of the protocol.
AI.method: Developed a protocol-aware tokenizer that splits along PDML dissector field trees, emits gap tokens for timing, and normalizes identifiers. Trained a compact 140M-parameter model on curated corpus of 802.11 traces.
AI.result: Achieves 74-97% next-packet token accuracy across five real-world failure categories and AUROC >= 0.99 for zero-shot anomaly detection. Comparable performance to frontier LLMs (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4) but with >600x fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: Learning from native protocol structure enables efficient foundation models for network analysis. Plume's compact size allows on-premise, privacy-preserving deployment with effectively zero marginal cost versus cloud APIs, enabling practical root cause analysis.

title: OpenHospital: A Thing-in-itself Arena for Evolving and Benchmarking LLM-based Collective Intelligence
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based Collective Intelligence (CI) presents a promising approach to overcoming the data wall and continuously boosting the capabilities of LLM agents. However, there is currently no dedicated arena for evolving and benchmarking LLM-based CI. To address this gap, we introduce OpenHospital, an interactive arena where physician agents can evolve CI through interactions with patient agents. This arena employs a data-in-agent-self paradigm that rapidly enhances agent capabilities and provides robust evaluation metrics for benchmarking both medical proficiency and system efficiency. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of OpenHospital in both fostering and quantifying CI.

AI.tldr: OpenHospital is an interactive arena for evolving and benchmarking Large Language Model-based Collective Intelligence through physician agents interacting with patient agents
AI.motivation: LLM-based Collective Intelligence could overcome data limitations and boost LLM agent capabilities, but lacks dedicated platforms for evolution and benchmarking
AI.method: Created OpenHospital arena with physician and patient agents using data-in-agent-self paradigm to enhance agent capabilities and provide evaluation metrics
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate OpenHospital effectively fosters and quantifies Collective Intelligence in medical proficiency and system efficiency
AI.conclusion: OpenHospital addresses the gap in LLM-based CI evolution and benchmarking, showing promise for advancing LLM agent capabilities through interactive arenas

title: PDE-SSM: A Spectral State Space Approach to Spatial Mixing in Diffusion Transformers
summary: The success of vision transformers-especially for generative modeling-is limited by the quadratic cost and weak spatial inductive bias of self-attention. We propose PDE-SSM, a spatial state-space block that replaces attention with a learnable convection-diffusion-reaction partial differential equation. This operator encodes a strong spatial prior by modeling information flow via physically grounded dynamics rather than all-to-all token interactions. Solving the PDE in the Fourier domain yields global coupling with near-linear complexity of $O(N \log N)$, delivering a principled and scalable alternative to attention. We integrate PDE-SSM into a flow-matching generative model to obtain the PDE-based Diffusion Transformer PDE-SSM-DiT. Empirically, PDE-SSM-DiT matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art Diffusion Transformers while substantially reducing compute. Our results show that, analogous to 1D settings where SSMs supplant attention, multi-dimensional PDE operators provide an efficient, inductive-bias-rich foundation for next-generation vision models.

AI.tldr: PDE-SSM replaces attention with a learnable convection-diffusion-reaction PDE for spatial processing, achieving near-linear complexity and strong spatial inductive bias, leading to efficient generative vision models that match or exceed state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Vision transformers suffer from quadratic self-attention cost and weak spatial inductive bias, limiting their efficiency and effectiveness for generative modeling tasks.
AI.method: Propose PDE-SSM, a spatial state-space block that replaces attention with a learnable convection-diffusion-reaction partial differential equation, solved in Fourier domain for global coupling with O(N log N) complexity.
AI.result: PDE-SSM-DiT (integrated into flow-matching generative model) matches or exceeds state-of-the-art Diffusion Transformers while substantially reducing computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Multi-dimensional PDE operators provide an efficient, inductive-bias-rich foundation for next-generation vision models, analogous to how SSMs replaced attention in 1D settings.

title: Knowledge Activation: AI Skills as the Institutional Knowledge Primitive for Agentic Software Development
summary:
Enterprise software organizations accumulate critical institutional knowledge - architectural decisions, deployment procedures, compliance policies, incident playbooks - yet this knowledge remains trapped in formats designed for human interpretation. The bottleneck to effective agentic software development is not model capability but knowledge architecture. When any knowledge consumer - an autonomous AI agent, a newly onboarded engineer, or a senior developer - encounters an enterprise task without institutional context, the result is guesswork, correction cascades, and a disproportionate tax on senior engineers who must manually supply what others cannot infer.
  This paper introduces Knowledge Activation, a framework that specializes AI Skills - the open standard for agent-consumable knowledge - into structured, governance-aware Atomic Knowledge Units (AKUs) for institutional knowledge delivery. Rather than retrieving documents for interpretation, AKUs deliver action - ready specifications encoding what to do, which tools to use, what constraints to respect, and where to go next - so that agents act correctly and engineers receive institutionally grounded guidance without reconstructing organizational context from scratch.
  AKUs form a composable knowledge graph that agents traverse at runtime - compressing onboarding, reducing cross - team friction, and eliminating correction cascades. The paper formalizes the resource constraints that make this architecture necessary, specifies the AKU schema and deployment architecture, and grounds long - term maintenance in knowledge commons practice. Organizations that architect their institutional knowledge for the agentic era will outperform those that invest solely in model capability.

AI.tldr: Knowledge Activation framework transforms institutional knowledge into structured Atomic Knowledge Units (AKUs) - executable specifications that enable AI agents to act correctly without human interpretation bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: Enterprise organizations have critical institutional knowledge trapped in human-readable formats, creating bottlenecks for AI agents and engineers. Without access to this context, tasks lead to guesswork, correction cascades, and excessive burden on senior engineers.
AI.method: The Knowledge Activation framework converts AI Skills (an open standard for agent-consumable knowledge) into structured, governance-aware Atomic Knowledge Units (AKUs). AKUs provide action-ready specifications that encode what to do, which tools to use, constraints to respect, and next steps.
AI.result: AKUs form a composable knowledge graph that agents can traverse at runtime, compressing onboarding time, reducing cross-team friction, and eliminating correction cascades in enterprise software development.
AI.conclusion: Organizations that architect their institutional knowledge for the agentic era using frameworks like Knowledge Activation will outperform those focusing solely on model capability, with knowledge architecture being the critical bottleneck rather than AI model capabilities.

title: Locally Linear Continual Learning for Time Series based on VC-Theoretical Generalization Bounds
comment: 12 pages. Accepted at IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
summary: Most machine learning methods assume fixed probability distributions, limiting their applicability in nonstationary real-world scenarios. While continual learning methods address this issue, current approaches often rely on black-box models or require extensive user intervention for interpretability. We propose SyMPLER (Systems Modeling through Piecewise Linear Evolving Regression), an explainable model for time series forecasting in nonstationary environments based on dynamic piecewise-linear approximations. Unlike other locally linear models, SyMPLER uses generalization bounds from Statistical Learning Theory to automatically determine when to add new local models based on prediction errors, eliminating the need for explicit clustering of the data. Experiments show that SyMPLER can achieve comparable performance to both black-box and existing explainable models while maintaining a human-interpretable structure that reveals insights about the system's behavior. In this sense, our approach conciliates accuracy and interpretability, offering a transparent and adaptive solution for forecasting nonstationary time series.

AI.tldr: SyMPLER is an explainable model for time series forecasting in nonstationary environments that uses dynamic piecewise-linear approximations with automatic model selection based on prediction errors, balancing accuracy and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Current machine learning methods assume fixed distributions, limiting applicability in nonstationary real-world scenarios. Continual learning approaches often rely on black-box models or require extensive user intervention for interpretability. There's a need for transparent, adaptive solutions for forecasting nonstationary time series.
AI.method: Proposes SyMPLER (Systems Modeling through Piecewise Linear Evolving Regression), an explainable model based on dynamic piecewise-linear approximations. Uses generalization bounds from Statistical Learning Theory to automatically determine when to add new local models based on prediction errors, eliminating the need for explicit data clustering.
AI.result: Experiments show SyMPLER achieves comparable performance to both black-box and existing explainable models while maintaining human-interpretable structure that reveals insights about system behavior.
AI.conclusion: SyMPLER reconciles accuracy and interpretability, offering a transparent and adaptive solution for forecasting nonstationary time series, addressing limitations of current methods in handling distribution shifts while maintaining explainability.

title: Planning as Goal Recognition: Deriving Heuristics from Intention Models - Extended Version
comment: Extended version of our paper accepted at ICAPS 2026
summary: Classical planning aims to find a sequence of actions, a plan, that maps a starting state into one of the goal states. If a trajectory appears to be leading to the goal, should we prioritise exploring it? Seminal work in goal recognition (GR) has defined GR in terms of a classical planning problem, adopting classical solvers and heuristics to recognise plans. We come full circle, and study the adoption and properties of GR-derived heuristics for seeking solutions to classical planning problems. We propose a new framework for assessing goal intention, which informs a new class of efficiently-computable heuristics. As a proof of concept, we derive two such heuristics, and show that they can already yield improvements for top-scoring classical planners. Our work provides foundational knowledge for understanding and deriving probabilistic intention-based heuristics for planning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel approach where goal recognition (GR) techniques are used to create new heuristics for classical planning, creating a circular relationship between planning and goal recognition where each informs the other.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to leverage insights from goal recognition research to improve classical planning performance. Since goal recognition has traditionally used planning techniques, the authors propose completing the cycle by using GR-derived concepts to enhance planning itself, particularly by providing heuristics that can better prioritize promising trajectories toward goals.
AI.method: The method involves creating a new framework for assessing goal intention, which enables the development of efficiently-computable heuristics. As proof of concept, the authors derive two specific heuristics from this framework and integrate them into classical planning algorithms to evaluate their effectiveness.
AI.result: The results show that the proposed GR-derived heuristics can yield improvements for top-scoring classical planners, demonstrating the practical value of this circular relationship between planning and goal recognition.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes foundational knowledge for understanding and deriving probabilistic intention-based heuristics for planning, creating a reciprocal relationship where planning informs goal recognition and goal recognition techniques can now enhance planning performance.

title: Quantum-Enhanced Vision Transformer for Flood Detection using Remote Sensing Imagery
summary: Reliable flood detection is critical for disaster management, yet classical deep learning models often struggle with the high-dimensional, nonlinear complexities inherent in remote sensing data. To mitigate these limitations, we introduced a novel Quantum-Enhanced Vision Transformer (ViT) that synergizes the global context-awareness of transformers with the expressive feature extraction capabilities of quantum computing. Using remote sensing imagery, we developed a hybrid architecture that processes inputs through parallel pathways, a ViT backbone and a quantum branch utilizing a 4-qubit parameterized quantum circuit for localized feature mapping. These distinct representations were fused to optimize binary classification. Results showed that the proposed hybrid model significantly outperformed a classical ViT baseline, increased overall accuracy from 84.48% to 94.47% and the F1-score from 0.841 to 0.944. Notably, the quantum integration substantially improved discriminative power in complex terrains for both class. These findings validate the potential of quantum-classical hybrid models to enhance precision in hydrological monitoring and earth observation applications.

AI.tldr: Novel Quantum-Enhanced Vision Transformer outperforms classical ViT for flood detection in remote sensing by combining transformer architecture with quantum computing for feature extraction.
AI.motivation: Classical deep learning models struggle with high-dimensional, nonlinear complexities in remote sensing data, requiring better approaches for reliable flood detection critical for disaster management.
AI.method: Developed hybrid Quantum-Enhanced Vision Transformer architecture with parallel processing: a ViT backbone for global context and a quantum branch using 4-qubit parameterized quantum circuit for localized feature mapping, then fused representations for binary classification.
AI.result: Significantly outperformed classical ViT baseline: increased overall accuracy from 84.48% to 94.47% and F1-score from 0.841 to 0.944. Quantum integration notably improved discriminative power in complex terrains for both classes.
AI.conclusion: The findings validate quantum-classical hybrid models' potential to enhance precision in hydrological monitoring and earth observation applications, demonstrating practical advantages for remote sensing tasks.

title: A Self-Evolving Defect Detection Framework for Industrial Photovoltaic Systems
summary: Reliable photovoltaic (PV) power generation requires timely detection of module defects that may reduce energy yield, accelerate degradation, and increase lifecycle operation and maintenance costs during field operation. Electroluminescence (EL) imaging has therefore been widely adopted for PV module inspection. However, automated defect detection in real operational environments remains challenging due to heterogeneous module geometries, low-resolution imaging conditions, subtle defect morphology, long-tailed defect distributions, and continual data shifts introduced by evolving inspection and labeling processes. These factors significantly limit the robustness and long-term maintainability of conventional deep-learning inspection pipelines. To address these challenges, this paper proposes SEPDD, a Self-Evolving Photovoltaic Defect Detection framework designed for evolving industrial PV inspection scenarios. SEPDD integrates automated model optimization with a continual self-evolving learning mechanism, enabling the inspection system to progressively adapt to distribution shifts and newly emerging defect patterns during long-term deployment. Experiments conducted on both a public PV defect benchmark and a private industrial EL dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. Both datasets exhibit severe class imbalance and significant domain shift. SEPDD achieves a leading mAP50 of 91.4% on the public dataset and 49.5% on the private dataset. It surpasses the autonomous baseline by 14.8% and human experts by 4.7% on the public dataset, and by 4.9% and 2.5%, respectively, on the private dataset.

AI.tldr: SEPDD is a Self-Evolving Photovoltaic Defect Detection framework that uses continual learning to adapt to distribution shifts and emerging defect patterns in industrial PV inspection environments, outperforming both autonomous baselines and human experts.
AI.motivation: Automated defect detection in photovoltaic modules using electroluminescence imaging faces challenges in real operational environments due to heterogeneous module geometries, low-resolution imaging, subtle defect morphology, long-tailed defect distributions, and continual data shifts from evolving inspection processes. These factors limit the robustness and long-term maintainability of conventional deep-learning inspection pipelines.
AI.method: The paper proposes SEPDD (Self-Evolving Photovoltaic Defect Detection framework) that integrates automated model optimization with a continual self-evolving learning mechanism. This enables the inspection system to progressively adapt to distribution shifts and newly emerging defect patterns during long-term deployment.
AI.result: SEPDD achieves mAP50 of 91.4% on public dataset (surpassing autonomous baseline by 14.8% and human experts by 4.7%) and 49.5% on private dataset (surpassing autonomous baseline by 4.9% and human experts by 2.5%). Both datasets exhibit severe class imbalance and significant domain shift.
AI.conclusion: SEPDD effectively addresses challenges in industrial PV inspection by enabling self-evolving adaptation to distribution shifts and emerging defect patterns, demonstrating superior performance over both automated baselines and human experts in realistic operational scenarios.

title: Routing Channel-Patch Dependencies in Time Series Forecasting with Graph Spectral Decomposition
comment: The Paper has been Accepted by ICLR 2026
summary: Time series forecasting has attracted significant attention in the field of AI. Previous works have revealed that the Channel-Independent (CI) strategy improves forecasting performance by modeling each channel individually, but it often suffers from poor generalization and overlooks meaningful inter-channel interactions. Conversely, Channel-Dependent (CD) strategies aggregate all channels, which may introduce irrelevant information and lead to oversmoothing. Despite recent progress, few existing methods offer the flexibility to adaptively balance CI and CD strategies in response to varying channel dependencies. To address this, we propose a generic plugin xCPD, that can adaptively model the channel-patch dependencies from the perspective of graph spectral decomposition. Specifically, xCPD first projects multivariate signals into the frequency domain using a shared graph Fourier basis, and groups patches into low-, mid-, and high-frequency bands based on their spectral energy responses. xCPD then applies a channel-adaptive routing mechanism that dynamically adjusts the degree of inter-channel interaction for each patch, enabling selective activation of frequency-specific experts. This facilitates fine-grained input-aware modeling of smooth trends, local fluctuations, and abrupt transitions. xCPD can be seamlessly integrated on top of existing CI and CD forecasting models, consistently enhancing both accuracy and generalization across benchmarks. The code is available https://github.com/Clearloveyuan/xCPD.

AI.tldr: xCPD is a plug-in module that adaptively balances channel-independent and channel-dependent strategies by modeling channel-patch dependencies through graph spectral decomposition and frequency-specific routing.
AI.motivation: Existing methods either use Channel-Independent (CI) strategies that overlook inter-channel interactions or Channel-Dependent (CD) strategies that introduce irrelevant information and cause oversmoothing. There's a lack of flexible methods that can adaptively balance CI and CD approaches based on varying channel dependencies.
AI.method: xCPD projects multivariate signals into frequency domain using shared graph Fourier basis, groups patches into low-, mid-, and high-frequency bands based on spectral energy responses, and applies a channel-adaptive routing mechanism to dynamically adjust inter-channel interaction for each patch. This enables selective activation of frequency-specific experts for different temporal patterns.
AI.result: xCPD can be seamlessly integrated with existing CI and CD forecasting models and consistently enhances both accuracy and generalization across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: xCPD provides a flexible solution for adaptively balancing channel-independent and channel-dependent strategies through graph spectral decomposition, enabling better modeling of channel-patch dependencies and improving time series forecasting performance.

title: A Hybrid AI and Rule-Based Decision Support System for Disease Diagnosis and Management Using Labs
summary: This research paper outlines the development and implementation of a novel Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) that integrates AI predictive modeling with medical knowledge bases. It utilizes the quantifiable information elements in lab results for inferring likely diagnoses a patient might have. Subsequently, suggesting investigations to confirm the likely diagnoses -- an assistive tool for physicians. The system fuses knowledge contained in a rule-base expert system with inferences of data driven predictors based on the features in labs. The data for 593,055 patients was collected from 547 primary care centers across the US to model our decision support system and derive Real-Word Evidence (RWE) to make it relevant for a large demographic of patients. Our Rule-Base comprises clinically validated rules, modeling 59 health conditions that can directly confirm one or more of diseases and assign ICD-10 codes to them. The Likely Diagnosis system uses multi-class classification, covering 37 ICD-10 codes, which are grouped together into 11 categories based on the labs that physicians prescribe to confirm the diagnosis. This research offers a novel system that assists a physician by utilizing medical profile of a patient and routine lab investigations to predict a group of likely diseases and then confirm them, coupled with providing explanations for inferences, thereby assisting physicians to reduce misdiagnosis of patients in clinical decision-making.

AI.tldr: A novel AI-driven Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) combines rule-based expert knowledge with predictive modeling using patient lab data to suggest likely diagnoses and confirmatory investigations, aiming to reduce misdiagnosis.
AI.motivation: To assist physicians in clinical decision-making by reducing misdiagnosis through intelligent analysis of patient medical profiles and routine lab results, combining clinical expertise with AI predictions.
AI.method: The system integrates a rule-based expert system with data-driven predictive models. It uses: 1) A rule-base with clinically validated rules covering 59 health conditions for direct disease confirmation and ICD-10 coding, 2) A Likely Diagnosis system using multi-class classification covering 37 ICD-10 codes grouped into 11 categories based on lab tests, 3) Analysis of quantifiable lab results from 593,055 patients across 547 US primary care centers to derive Real-World Evidence.
AI.result: Developed a comprehensive CDSS that can: 1) Predict groups of likely diseases based on patient medical profiles and lab results, 2) Confirm diagnoses using rule-based validation, 3) Suggest further investigations to confirm likely diagnoses, 4) Provide explanations for inferences, 5) Cover a wide demographic through analysis of large-scale real-world patient data.
AI.conclusion: The research presents an innovative assistive tool that successfully combines clinical knowledge bases with AI predictive modeling to support physicians in diagnosis, potentially reducing misdiagnosis by leveraging both expert rules and data-driven insights from extensive patient lab data.

title: Data-driven Progressive Discovery of Physical Laws
summary: Symbolic regression is a powerful tool for knowledge discovery, enabling the extraction of interpretable mathematical expressions directly from data. However, conventional symbolic discovery typically follows an end-to-end, "one-step" process, which often generates lengthy and physically meaningless expressions when dealing with real physical systems, leading to poor model generalization. This limitation fundamentally stems from its deviation from the basic path of scientific discovery: physical laws do not exist in a single form but follow a hierarchical and progressive pattern from simplicity to complexity. Motivated by this principle, we propose Chain of Symbolic Regression (CoSR), a novel framework that models the discovery of physical laws as a chain of symbolic knowledge. This knowledge chain is formed by progressively combining multiple knowledge units with clear physical meanings along a specific logic, ultimately enabling the precise discovery of the underlying physical laws from data. CoSR fully recapitulates the progressive discovery path from Kepler's third law to the law of universal gravitation in classical mechanics, and is applied to three types of problems: turbulent Rayleigh-Benard convection, viscous flows in a circular pipe, and laser-metal interaction, demonstrating its ability to improve classical scaling theories. Finally, CoSR showcases its capability to discover new knowledge in the complex engineering problem of aerodynamic coefficients scaling for different aircraft.

AI.tldr: CoSR (Chain of Symbolic Regression) is a novel symbolic regression framework that models physical law discovery as a progressive chain of symbolic knowledge units, mimicking hierarchical scientific discovery from simple to complex patterns.
AI.motivation: Conventional symbolic regression uses an end-to-end approach that often generates lengthy, physically meaningless expressions with poor generalization when applied to real physical systems. This limitation arises because it deviates from the natural scientific discovery process where physical laws follow hierarchical, progressive patterns from simplicity to complexity.
AI.method: CoSR models physical law discovery as a chain of symbolic knowledge units. It progressively combines multiple knowledge units with clear physical meanings along specific logical paths, forming a knowledge chain that enables precise discovery of underlying physical laws from data.
AI.result: CoSR successfully recapitulates the progressive discovery path from Kepler's third law to the law of universal gravitation. It demonstrates improved classical scaling theories in three applications: turbulent Rayleigh-Benard convection, viscous flows in circular pipes, and laser-metal interaction. Additionally, it discovers new knowledge for aerodynamic coefficients scaling across different aircraft.
AI.conclusion: CoSR provides a more effective framework for symbolic regression by mimicking the hierarchical nature of scientific discovery, enabling better generalization and more physically meaningful expressions across both classical physics problems and complex engineering applications.

title: RS-WorldModel: a Unified Model for Remote Sensing Understanding and Future Sense Forecasting
summary: Remote sensing world models aim to both explain observed changes and forecast plausible futures, two tasks that share spatiotemporal priors. Existing methods, however, typically address them separately, limiting cross-task transfer. We present RS-WorldModel, a unified world model for remote sensing that jointly handles spatiotemporal change understanding and text-guided future scene forecasting, and we build RSWBench-1.1M, a 1.1 million sample dataset with rich language annotations covering both tasks. RS-WorldModel is trained in three stages: (1) Geo-Aware Generative Pre-training (GAGP) conditions forecasting on geographic and acquisition metadata; (2) synergistic instruction tuning (SIT) jointly trains understanding and forecasting; (3) verifiable reinforcement optimization (VRO) refines outputs with verifiable, task-specific rewards. With only 2B parameters, RS-WorldModel surpasses open-source models up to 120$ \times $ larger on most spatiotemporal change question-answering metrics. It achieves an FID of 43.13 on text-guided future scene forecasting, outperforming all open-source baselines as well as the closed-source Gemini-2.5-Flash Image (Nano Banana).

AI.tldr: RS-WorldModel: A unified 2B-parameter model that jointly handles spatiotemporal change understanding and text-guided future scene forecasting in remote sensing, outperforming much larger models through a three-stage training approach.
AI.motivation: Existing methods typically address spatiotemporal change understanding and forecasting separately, limiting cross-task transfer despite these tasks sharing spatiotemporal priors. There's a need for a unified approach that can leverage shared knowledge between these related tasks.
AI.method: Three-stage training: 1) Geo-Aware Generative Pre-training (GAGP) conditions forecasting on geographic and acquisition metadata; 2) Synergistic instruction tuning (SIT) jointly trains understanding and forecasting tasks; 3) Verifiable reinforcement optimization (VRO) refines outputs with task-specific rewards. The model uses a new 1.1M sample dataset (RSWBench-1.1M) with rich language annotations.
AI.result: RS-WorldModel with only 2B parameters surpasses open-source models up to 120× larger on most spatiotemporal change question-answering metrics. Achieves FID of 43.13 on text-guided future scene forecasting, outperforming all open-source baselines and closed-source Gemini-2.5-Flash Image (Nano Banana).
AI.conclusion: RS-WorldModel demonstrates that unified world models can effectively handle both spatiotemporal change understanding and future forecasting in remote sensing, achieving state-of-the-art performance with relatively small parameter count through synergistic training and domain-specific adaptations.

title: Few Batches or Little Memory, But Not Both: Simultaneous Space and Adaptivity Constraints in Stochastic Bandits
summary:
We study stochastic multi-armed bandits under simultaneous constraints on space and adaptivity: the learner interacts with the environment in $B$ batches and has only $W$ bits of persistent memory. Prior work shows that each constraint alone is surprisingly mild: near-minimax regret $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{KT})$ is achievable with $O(\log T)$ bits of memory under fully adaptive interaction, and with a $K$-independent $O(\log\log T)$-type number of batches when memory is unrestricted. We show that this picture breaks down in the simultaneously constrained regime. We prove that any algorithm with a $W$-bit memory constraint must use at least $Ω(K/W)$ batches to achieve near-minimax regret $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{KT})$ , even under adaptive grids. In particular, logarithmic memory rules out $K$-independent batch complexity.
  Our proof is based on an information bottleneck. We show that near-minimax regret forces the learner to acquire $Ω(K)$ bits of information about the hidden set of good arms under a suitable hard prior, whereas an algorithm with $B$ batches and $W$ bits of memory allows only $O(BW)$ bits of information. A key ingredient is a localized change-of-measure lemma that yields probability-level arm exploration guarantees, which is of independent interest. We also give an algorithm using $O(\log T)$ bits of memory and $\widetilde{O}(K)$ batches that achieves regret $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{KT})$, which nearly matches our lower bound.

AI.tldr: Simultaneous constraints on memory (W bits) and adaptivity (B batches) in multi-armed bandits require Ω(K/W) batches for near-minimax regret, showing logarithmic memory prevents K-independent batch complexity, with matching upper bounds provided.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed that memory constraints (W bits) and adaptivity constraints (B batches) individually are mild for achieving near-minimax regret in stochastic multi-armed bandits. The paper investigates what happens when both constraints are imposed simultaneously, which had not been studied before.
AI.method: The authors use information-theoretic techniques, establishing an information bottleneck argument. They prove a lower bound showing that with W-bit memory, any algorithm needs Ω(K/W) batches to achieve near-minimax regret. A key technical contribution is a localized change-of-measure lemma for probability-level arm exploration guarantees. They also provide a matching upper bound algorithm using O(log T) bits of memory and Õ(K) batches.
AI.result: The main result is that simultaneous constraints fundamentally change the problem landscape: logarithmic memory (O(log T)) rules out K-independent batch complexity, requiring Ω(K/W) batches for near-minimax regret. They provide an algorithm achieving Õ(√KT) regret with O(log T) memory and Õ(K) batches, nearly matching the lower bound.
AI.conclusion: The simultaneous imposition of memory and adaptivity constraints creates a tradeoff not present when considering either constraint alone, establishing a fundamental Ω(K/W) batch requirement for near-minimax performance, with matching upper bounds demonstrating this tradeoff is essentially tight.

title: Manifold-Orthogonal Dual-spectrum Extrapolation for Parameterized Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have achieved notable success in modeling dynamical systems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs). To avoid computationally expensive retraining under new physical conditions, parameterized PINNs (P$^2$INNs) commonly adapt pre-trained operators using singular value decomposition (SVD) for out-of-distribution (OOD) regimes. However, SVD-based fine-tuning often suffers from rigid subspace locking and truncation of important high-frequency spectral modes, limiting its ability to capture complex physical transitions. While parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods appear to be promising alternatives, applying conventional adapters such as LoRA to P$^2$INNs introduces a severe Pareto trade-off, as additive updates increase parameter overhead and disrupt the structured physical manifolds inherent in operator representations. To address these limitations, we propose Manifold-Orthogonal Dual-spectrum Extrapolation (MODE), a lightweight micro-architecture designed for physics operator adaptation. MODE decomposes physical evolution into complementary mechanisms including principal-spectrum dense mixing that enables cross-modal energy transfer within frozen orthogonal bases, residual-spectrum awakening that activates high-frequency spectral components through a single trainable scalar, and affine Galilean unlocking that explicitly isolates spatial translation dynamics. Experiments on challenging PDE benchmarks including the 1D Convection--Diffusion--Reaction equation and the 2D Helmholtz equation demonstrate that MODE achieves strong out-of-distribution generalization while preserving the minimal parameter complexity of native SVD and outperforming existing PEFT-based baselines.

AI.tldr: MODE: A lightweight PEFT method for PINNs that enables OOD generalization without disrupting physics manifolds, outperforming SVD and other PEFT approaches.
AI.motivation: Current P²INNs using SVD-based fine-tuning suffer from rigid subspace locking and truncation of high-frequency modes, limiting OOD adaptation. Conventional PEFT methods like LoRA introduce parameter overhead and disrupt structured physical manifolds in operator representations.
AI.method: Proposes Manifold-Orthogonal Dual-spectrum Extrapolation (MODE) with three mechanisms: principal-spectrum dense mixing for cross-modal energy transfer within frozen bases, residual-spectrum awakening using a single trainable scalar to activate high-frequency components, and affine Galilean unlocking to isolate spatial translation dynamics.
AI.result: MODE achieves strong OOD generalization on challenging PDE benchmarks (1D Convection-Diffusion-Reaction and 2D Helmholtz equations) while maintaining minimal parameter complexity comparable to SVD, outperforming existing PEFT baselines.
AI.conclusion: MODE provides an effective lightweight adaptation framework for physics-informed neural operators that balances parameter efficiency, physics-preserving structure, and strong out-of-distribution generalization capability.

title: Exposing Cross-Modal Consistency for Fake News Detection in Short-Form Videos
summary: Short-form video platforms are major channels for news but also fertile ground for multimodal misinformation where each modality appears plausible alone yet cross-modal relationships are subtly inconsistent, like mismatched visuals and captions. On two benchmark datasets, FakeSV (Chinese) and FakeTT (English), we observe a clear asymmetry: real videos exhibit high text-visual but moderate text-audio consistency, while fake videos show the opposite pattern. Moreover, a single global consistency score forms an interpretable axis along which fake probability and prediction errors vary smoothly. Motivated by these observations, we present MAGIC3 (Modal-Adversarial Gated Interaction and Consistency-Centric Classifier), a detector that explicitly models and exposes cross-tri-modal consistency signals at multiple granularities. MAGIC3 combines explicit pairwise and global consistency modeling with token- and frame-level consistency signals derived from cross-modal attention, incorporates multi-style LLM rewrites to obtain style-robust text representations, and employs an uncertainty-aware classifier for selective VLM routing. Using pre-extracted features, MAGIC3 consistently outperforms the strongest non-VLM baselines on FakeSV and FakeTT. While matching VLM-level accuracy, the two-stage system achieves 18-27x higher throughput and 93% VRAM savings, offering a strong cost-performance tradeoff.

AI.tldr: MAGIC3 is an efficient fake video detector that exploits cross-modal consistency patterns to distinguish real vs. fake short-form videos while achieving VLM-level accuracy with much higher throughput and lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: Short-form video platforms are major news sources but vulnerable to multimodal misinformation where each modality appears plausible individually but contains subtle cross-modal inconsistencies (like mismatched visuals and captions). Real vs. fake videos exhibit distinct cross-modal consistency patterns that can be exploited for detection.
AI.method: MAGIC3 (Modal-Adversarial Gated Interaction and Consistency-Centric Classifier) explicitly models cross-tri-modal consistency at multiple granularities: 1) Explicit pairwise and global consistency modeling, 2) Token- and frame-level consistency signals from cross-modal attention, 3) Multi-style LLM rewrites for style-robust text representations, 4) Uncertainty-aware classifier for selective VLM routing, and 5) Using pre-extracted features for efficiency.
AI.result: On FakeSV (Chinese) and FakeTT (English) datasets, MAGIC3 consistently outperforms strongest non-VLM baselines. While matching VLM-level accuracy, the two-stage system achieves 18-27x higher throughput and 93% VRAM savings, offering strong cost-performance tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal consistency patterns provide a valuable signal for detecting multimodal misinformation in short-form videos. MAGIC3 demonstrates that explicitly modeling these consistency relationships can achieve state-of-the-art detection performance while maintaining computational efficiency through careful system design and feature extraction strategies.

title: Level Up: Defining and Exploiting Transitional Problems for Curriculum Learning
summary: Curriculum learning--ordering training examples in a sequence to aid machine learning--takes inspiration from human learning, but has not gained widespread acceptance. Static strategies for scoring item difficulty rely on indirect proxy scores of varying quality and produce curricula that are not specific to the learner at hand. Dynamic approaches base difficulty estimates on gradient information, requiring considerable extra computation during training. We introduce a novel method for measuring the difficulty of individual problem instances directly relative to the ability of a given model, and identify transitional problems that are consistently easier as model ability increases. Applying this method to chess and mathematics, we find that training on a curriculum that "levels up" from easier to harder transitional problems most efficiently improves a model to the next tier of competence. These problems induce a natural progression from easier to harder items, which outperforms other training strategies. By measuring difficulty directly relative to model competence, our method yields interpretable problems, learner-specific curricula, and a principled basis for step-by-step improvement.

AI.tldr: A new method for curriculum learning that measures problem difficulty directly relative to a model's current ability, identifying "transitional problems" that provide optimal stepping stones for improvement.
AI.motivation: Current curriculum learning approaches have limitations: static strategies use proxy scores of varying quality and aren't learner-specific, while dynamic approaches require expensive gradient computations during training.
AI.method: The method measures difficulty of individual problem instances directly relative to the current model's ability, identifying transitional problems that are consistently easier as model ability increases.
AI.result: Applied to chess and mathematics, training on a curriculum that "levels up" from easier to harder transitional problems most efficiently improves models to the next tier of competence.
AI.conclusion: By measuring difficulty directly relative to model competence, this approach yields interpretable problems, learner-specific curricula, and a principled basis for step-by-step improvement.

title: Consequentialist Objectives and Catastrophe
summary:
Because human preferences are too complex to codify, AIs operate with misspecified objectives. Optimizing such objectives often produces undesirable outcomes; this phenomenon is known as reward hacking. Such outcomes are not necessarily catastrophic. Indeed, most examples of reward hacking in previous literature are benign. And typically, objectives can be modified to resolve the issue.
  We study the prospect of catastrophic outcomes induced by AIs operating in complex environments. We argue that, when capabilities are sufficiently advanced, pursuing a fixed consequentialist objective tends to result in catastrophic outcomes. We formalize this by establishing conditions that provably lead to such outcomes. Under these conditions, simple or random behavior is safe. Catastrophic risk arises due to extraordinary competence rather than incompetence.
  With a fixed consequentialist objective, avoiding catastrophe requires constraining AI capabilities. In fact, constraining capabilities the right amount not only averts catastrophe but yields valuable outcomes. Our results apply to any objective produced by modern industrial AI development pipelines.

AI.tldr: AI systems pursuing fixed consequentialist objectives in complex environments can cause catastrophic outcomes when capabilities advance, requiring capability constraints to prevent reward hacking disasters.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the risk of catastrophic outcomes from AI systems operating with misspecified objectives in complex environments. Current AI development focuses on pursuing fixed objectives, but as capabilities advance, this approach can lead to dangerous reward hacking scenarios where AIs find harmful ways to optimize their objectives.
AI.method: The authors formalize the problem by establishing provable conditions under which catastrophic outcomes occur. They analyze the relationship between AI capabilities, fixed consequentialist objectives, and environmental complexity to demonstrate that simple or random behavior is safe, while extraordinary competence with fixed objectives leads to catastrophe.
AI.result: The analysis shows that when AI capabilities become sufficiently advanced while pursuing fixed consequentialist objectives in complex environments, catastrophic outcomes are inevitable. The paper demonstrates that constraining capabilities appropriately can not only prevent catastrophe but also yield valuable outcomes, and these findings apply universally to objectives produced by modern industrial AI development pipelines.
AI.conclusion: To avoid catastrophic outcomes from advanced AI systems, we must either avoid fixed consequentialist objectives or appropriately constrain AI capabilities. Catastrophic risk emerges from extraordinary competence rather than incompetence, and simple/random behavior remains safe. Current industrial AI development approaches are fundamentally vulnerable to this risk.

title: Greedy Information Projection for LLM Data Selection
comment: Published as a paper at 3rd DATA-FM workshop @ ICLR 2026, Brazil
summary: We present \emph{Greedy Information Projection} (\textsc{GIP}), a principled framework for choosing training examples for large language model fine-tuning. \textsc{GIP} casts selection as maximizing mutual information between a subset of examples and task-specific query signals, which may originate from LLM quality judgments, metadata, or other sources. The framework involves optimizing a closed-form mutual information objective defined using both data and query embeddings, naturally balancing {\it quality} and {\it diversity}. Optimizing this score is equivalent to maximizing the projection of the query embedding matrix onto the span of the selected data, which provides a geometric explanation for the co-emergence of quality and diversity. Building on this view, we employ a fast greedy matching-pursuit procedure with efficient projection-based updates. On instruction-following and mathematical reasoning datasets, \textsc{GIP} selects small subsets that match full-data fine-tuning while using only a fraction of examples and compute, unifying quality-aware and diversity-aware selection for efficient fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: GIP is a mutual information-based framework for selecting optimal training examples for LLM fine-tuning, balancing quality and diversity through efficient greedy optimization.
AI.motivation: Need principled framework for selecting training examples for efficient LLM fine-tuning that balances both quality and diversity without requiring full dataset training.
AI.method: Maximizes mutual information between selected examples and task-specific queries using closed-form objective, optimized via greedy matching-pursuit with efficient projection updates.
AI.result: GIP selects small subsets that match full-data fine-tuning performance on instruction-following and mathematical reasoning tasks using only fraction of examples/compute.
AI.conclusion: GIP provides principled, efficient framework unifying quality and diversity selection for LLM fine-tuning with strong theoretical foundation and practical performance.

title: VTC-Bench: Evaluating Agentic Multimodal Models via Compositional Visual Tool Chaining
summary: Recent advancements extend Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) beyond standard visual question answering to utilizing external tools for advanced visual tasks. Despite this progress, precisely executing and effectively composing diverse tools for complex tasks remain persistent bottleneck. Constrained by sparse tool-sets and simple tool-use trajectories, existing benchmarks fail to capture complex and diverse tool interactions, falling short in evaluating model performance under practical, real-world conditions. To bridge this gap, we introduce VisualToolChain-Bench~(VTC-Bench), a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate tool-use proficiency in MLLMs. To align with realistic computer vision pipelines, our framework features 32 diverse OpenCV-based visual operations. This rich tool-set enables extensive combinations, allowing VTC-Bench to rigorously assess multi-tool composition and long-horizon, multi-step plan execution. For precise evaluation, we provide 680 curated problems structured across a nine-category cognitive hierarchy, each with ground-truth execution trajectories. Extensive experiments on 19 leading MLLMs reveal critical limitations in current models' visual agentic capabilities. Specifically, models struggle to adapt to diverse tool-sets and generalize to unseen operations, with the leading model Gemini-3.0-Pro only achieving 51\% on our benchmark. Furthermore, multi-tool composition remains a persistent challenge. When facing complex tasks, models struggle to formulate efficient execution plans, relying heavily on a narrow, suboptimal subset of familiar functions rather than selecting the optimal tools. By identifying these fundamental challenges, VTC-Bench establishes a rigorous baseline to guide the development of more generalized visual agentic models.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark called VTC-Bench is introduced to evaluate how well multimodal LLMs can use visual tools for complex tasks, revealing current models struggle with diverse tool selection and composition.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks fail to capture the complexity of real-world visual tasks because they use sparse tool-sets and simple tool trajectories, making them inadequate for evaluating practical tool-use capabilities in MLLMs.
AI.method: The researchers created VTC-Bench with 32 diverse OpenCV-based visual operations that can be extensively combined, and 680 curated problems structured across a nine-category cognitive hierarchy with ground-truth execution trajectories.
AI.result: Evaluation of 19 leading MLLMs shows critical limitations: models struggle to adapt to diverse tool-sets and generalize to unseen operations (Gemini-3.0-Pro achieved only 51%), and they fail at multi-tool composition for complex tasks.
AI.conclusion: VTC-Bench establishes a rigorous baseline that identifies fundamental challenges in visual agentic capabilities, particularly around tool adaptation, generalization, and composition, guiding future development of more generalized visual agentic models.

title: IGU-LoRA: Adaptive Rank Allocation via Integrated Gradients and Uncertainty-Aware Scoring
summary: As large language models (LLMs) scale to billions of parameters, full-parameter fine-tuning becomes compute- and memory-prohibitive. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) mitigates this issue by updating only a small set of task-specific parameters while keeping the base model frozen. Among PEFT approaches, low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is widely adopted; however, it enforces a uniform rank across layers despite substantial variation in layer importance, motivating {layerwise} rank allocation. Recent adaptive-rank variants (e.g., AdaLoRA) allocate ranks based on importance scores, yet typically rely on instantaneous gradients that capture only local sensitivity, overlooking non-local, pathwise effects within the same layer, which yields unstable and biased scores. To address this limitation, we introduce IGU-LoRA, an adaptive-rank LoRA that (i) computes within-layer Integrated Gradients (IG) sensitivities and aggregates them into a layer-level score for rank allocation, and (ii) applies an uncertainty-aware scheme using exponential moving averages with deviation tracking to suppress noisy updates and calibrate rank selection. Theoretically, we prove an upper bound on the composite trapezoidal rule approximation error for parameter-space IG under a pathwise Hessian-Lipschitz condition, which informs the quadrature budget. Across diverse tasks and architectures, IGU-LoRA consistently outperforms strong PEFT baselines at matched parameter budgets, improving downstream accuracy and robustness. Ablations confirm the contributions of pathwise within-layer sensitivity estimates and uncertainty-aware selection to effective rank allocation. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/withyou12/igulora.git

AI.tldr: IGU-LoRA: An adaptive-rank LoRA method that uses Integrated Gradients and uncertainty-aware selection for better layerwise rank allocation in parameter-efficient fine-tuning of LLMs.
AI.motivation: Full-parameter fine-tuning of billion-parameter LLMs is computationally prohibitive. While LoRA is popular for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, it uses uniform rank across layers despite layer importance variation. Existing adaptive-rank methods (like AdaLoRA) use instantaneous gradients that only capture local sensitivity, ignoring non-local pathwise effects within layers, leading to unstable and biased importance scores.
AI.method: IGU-LoRA computes within-layer Integrated Gradients (IG) sensitivities, aggregates them into layer-level scores for rank allocation, and uses an uncertainty-aware scheme with exponential moving averages and deviation tracking to suppress noise and calibrate rank selection. Theoretically analyzes approximation error for parameter-space IG under Hessian-Lipschitz conditions.
AI.result: IGU-LoRA consistently outperforms strong PEFT baselines across diverse tasks and architectures at matched parameter budgets, improving downstream accuracy and robustness. Ablations confirm contributions of pathwise within-layer sensitivity estimates and uncertainty-aware selection.
AI.conclusion: IGU-LoRA provides a more effective adaptive-rank LoRA approach by capturing non-local sensitivity through Integrated Gradients and stabilizing allocation with uncertainty-aware methods, offering superior performance for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models.

title: Prompt Readiness Levels (PRL): a maturity scale and scoring framework for production grade prompt assets
summary: Prompt engineering has become a production critical component of generative AI systems. However, organizations still lack a shared, auditable method to qualify prompt assets against operational objectives, safety constraints, and compliance requirements. This paper introduces Prompt Readiness Levels (PRL), a nine level maturity scale inspired by TRL, and the Prompt Readiness Score (PRS), a multidimensional scoring method with gating thresholds designed to prevent weak link failure modes. PRL/PRS provide an original, structured and methodological framework for governing prompt assets specification, testing, traceability, security evaluation, and deployment readiness enabling valuation of prompt engineering through reproducible qualification decisions across teams and industries.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Prompt Readiness Levels (PRL) and Prompt Readiness Score (PRS) - a structured framework for qualifying and governing prompt engineering assets in generative AI systems to ensure operational, safety, and compliance requirements are met.
AI.motivation: Organizations lack a shared, auditable method to qualify prompt assets against operational objectives, safety constraints, and compliance requirements in generative AI systems, despite prompt engineering being a production-critical component.
AI.method: The authors introduce a two-part framework: 1) Prompt Readiness Levels (PRL) - a nine-level maturity scale inspired by Technology Readiness Levels (TRL), and 2) Prompt Readiness Score (PRS) - a multidimensional scoring method with gating thresholds designed to prevent weak link failure modes.
AI.result: PRL/PRS provides an original, structured framework for governing prompt assets specification, testing, traceability, security evaluation, and deployment readiness, enabling reproducible qualification decisions across teams and industries.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PRL/PRS framework enables organizations to systematically value and govern prompt engineering through standardized qualification methods, addressing the current lack of shared, auditable approaches for prompt asset readiness assessment in production AI systems.

title: Computation and Communication Efficient Federated Unlearning via On-server Gradient Conflict Mitigation and Expression
summary: Federated Unlearning (FUL) aims to remove specific participants' data contributions from a trained Federated Learning model, thereby ensuring data privacy and compliance with regulatory requirements. Despite its potential, progress in FUL has been limited due to several challenges, including the cross-client knowledge inaccessibility and high computational and communication costs. To overcome these challenges, we propose Federated On-server Unlearning (FOUL), a novel framework that comprises two key stages. The learning-to-unlearn stage serves as a preparatory learning phase, during which the model identifies and encodes the key features associated with the forget clients. This stage is communication-efficient and establishes the basis for the subsequent unlearning process. Subsequently, on-server knowledge aggregation phase aims to perform the unlearning process at the server without requiring access to client data, thereby preserving both efficiency and privacy. We introduce a new data setting for FUL, which enables a more transparent and rigorous evaluation of unlearning. To highlight the effectiveness of our approach, we propose a novel evaluation metric termed time-to-forget, which measures how quickly the model achieves optimal unlearning performance. Extensive experiments conducted on three datasets under various unlearning scenarios demonstrate that FOUL outperforms the Retraining in FUL. Moreover, FOUL achieves competitive or superior results with significantly reduced time-to-forget, while maintaining low communication and computation costs.

AI.tldr: Federated On-server Unlearning (FOUL) is a novel framework for Federated Unlearning that performs unlearning entirely on the server side without client data access, achieving efficient and effective data removal from federated models.
AI.motivation: Federated Unlearning is crucial for data privacy and regulatory compliance (like GDPR's right to be forgotten), but existing methods face challenges: cross-client knowledge inaccessibility, high computational/communication costs, and lack of transparent evaluation settings.
AI.method: FOUL uses a two-stage approach: (1) Learning-to-unlearn stage: model identifies and encodes key features of forget clients' data (communication-efficient preparatory phase). (2) On-server knowledge aggregation: performs unlearning at server without client data access, maintaining privacy and efficiency. Also introduces new evaluation metric "time-to-forget."
AI.result: FOUL outperforms retraining in Federated Unlearning across three datasets under various scenarios. Achieves competitive/superior results with significantly reduced time-to-forget while maintaining low communication and computation costs.
AI.conclusion: FOUL provides an effective solution for Federated Unlearning that addresses key challenges of privacy, efficiency, and evaluation transparency, enabling practical implementation of data removal in federated learning systems.

title: Interference-Aware K-Step Reachable Communication in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Effective communication is pivotal for addressing complex collaborative tasks in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). Yet, limited communication bandwidth and dynamic, intricate environmental topologies present significant challenges in identifying high-value communication partners. Agents must consequently select collaborators under uncertainty, lacking a priori knowledge of which partners can deliver task-critical information. To this end, we propose Interference-Aware K-Step Reachable Communication (IA-KRC), a novel framework that enhances cooperation via two core components: (1) a K-Step reachability protocol that confines message passing to physically accessible neighbors, and (2) an interference-prediction module that optimizes partner choice by minimizing interference while maximizing utility. Compared to existing methods, IA-KRC enables substantially more persistent and efficient cooperation despite environmental interference. Comprehensive evaluations confirm that IA-KRC achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines, while demonstrating enhanced robustness and scalability in complex topological and highly dynamic multi-agent scenarios.

AI.tldr: IA-KRC is a communication framework for MARL that uses K-step reachability and interference prediction to select optimal communication partners in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Limited bandwidth and dynamic topologies in multi-agent systems make it difficult to identify high-value communication partners, as agents must select collaborators without knowing which partners can provide task-critical information.
AI.method: Proposes Interference-Aware K-Step Reachable Communication (IA-KRC) with two components: (1) K-step reachability protocol restricting message passing to physically accessible neighbors, and (2) interference-prediction module optimizing partner choice by minimizing interference while maximizing utility.
AI.result: Achieves substantially more persistent and efficient cooperation despite environmental interference, with superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines in comprehensive evaluations.
AI.conclusion: IA-KRC demonstrates enhanced robustness and scalability in complex topological and highly dynamic multi-agent scenarios, effectively addressing communication challenges in MARL.

title: Node Role-Guided LLMs for Dynamic Graph Clustering
comment: The paper has been accepted by WWW 2026
summary: Dynamic graph clustering aims to detect and track time-varying clusters in dynamic graphs, revealing how complex real-world systems evolve over time. However, existing methods are predominantly black-box models. They lack interpretability in their clustering decisions and fail to provide semantic explanations of why clusters form or how they evolve, severely limiting their use in safety-critical domains such as healthcare or transportation. To address these limitations, we propose an end-to-end interpretable framework that maps continuous graph embeddings into discrete semantic concepts through learnable prototypes. Specifically, we first decompose node representations into orthogonal role and clustering subspaces, so that nodes with similar roles (e.g., hubs, bridges) but different cluster affiliations can be properly distinguished. We then introduce five node role prototypes (Leader, Contributor, Wanderer, Connector, Newcomer) in the role subspace as semantic anchors, transforming continuous embeddings into discrete concepts to facilitate LLM understanding of node roles within communities. Finally, we design a hierarchical LLM reasoning mechanism to generate both clustering results and natural language explanations, while providing consistency feedback as weak supervision to refine node representations. Experimental results on four synthetic and six real-world benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness, interpretability, and robustness of DyG-RoLLM. Code is available at https://github.com/Clearloveyuan/DyG-RoLLM.

AI.tldr: DyG-RoLLM is an interpretable dynamic graph clustering framework that uses learnable prototypes to map graph embeddings to discrete semantic concepts, enabling LLM-based reasoning for clustering and explanation generation.
AI.motivation: Existing dynamic graph clustering methods lack interpretability, making them unsuitable for safety-critical domains like healthcare and transportation where understanding why clusters form and evolve is crucial.
AI.method: 1) Decompose node representations into orthogonal role and clustering subspaces; 2) Introduce five node role prototypes (Leader, Contributor, Wanderer, Connector, Newcomer) as semantic anchors; 3) Design hierarchical LLM reasoning mechanism for clustering and natural language explanations with consistency feedback for refinement.
AI.result: Experimental results on four synthetic and six real-world benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness, interpretability, and robustness of DyG-RoLLM.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses interpretability limitations in dynamic graph clustering, enabling semantic understanding of cluster formation and evolution through LLM-powered reasoning.

title: PrototypeNAS: Rapid Design of Deep Neural Networks for Microcontroller Units
summary: Enabling efficient deep neural network (DNN) inference on edge devices with different hardware constraints is a challenging task that typically requires DNN architectures to be specialized for each device separately. To avoid the huge manual effort, one can use neural architecture search (NAS). However, many existing NAS methods are resource-intensive and time-consuming because they require the training of many different DNNs from scratch. Furthermore, they do not take the resource constraints of the target system into account. To address these shortcomings, we propose PrototypeNAS, a zero-shot NAS method to accelerate and automate the selection, compression, and specialization of DNNs to different target microcontroller units (MCUs). We propose a novel three-step search method that decouples DNN design and specialization from DNN training for a given target platform. First, we present a novel search space that not only cuts out smaller DNNs from a single large architecture, but instead combines the structural optimization of multiple architecture types, as well as optimization of their pruning and quantization configurations. Second, we explore the use of an ensemble of zero-shot proxies during optimization instead of a single one. Third, we propose the use of Hypervolume subset selection to distill DNN architectures from the Pareto front of the multi-objective optimization that represent the most meaningful tradeoffs between accuracy and FLOPs. We evaluate the effectiveness of PrototypeNAS on 12 different datasets in three different tasks: image classification, time series classification, and object detection. Our results demonstrate that PrototypeNAS is able to identify DNN models within minutes that are small enough to be deployed on off-the-shelf MCUs and still achieve accuracies comparable to the performance of large DNN models.

AI.tldr: PrototypeNAS is a zero-shot neural architecture search method that quickly identifies optimized DNN architectures for edge devices by combining structural optimization, pruning, and quantization without requiring full training.
AI.motivation: DNN inference on edge devices requires specialized architectures for different hardware constraints. Existing NAS methods are resource-intensive and time-consuming, requiring training many DNNs from scratch and often ignoring target system resource constraints.
AI.method: 1) Novel search space combining structural optimization of multiple architecture types with pruning and quantization configurations; 2) Ensemble of zero-shot proxies for optimization instead of single proxy; 3) Hypervolume subset selection to distill most meaningful Pareto-optimal DNNs from accuracy-FLOPs tradeoffs.
AI.result: PrototypeNAS identifies DNN models within minutes that are small enough for off-the-shelf MCUs while achieving accuracies comparable to large models. Validated on 12 datasets across image classification, time series classification, and object detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: PrototypeNAS provides an efficient, automated approach for selecting, compressing, and specializing DNNs for different MCU platforms without extensive training, addressing the challenges of edge deployment with diverse hardware constraints.

title: Prototypical Exemplar Condensation for Memory-efficient Online Continual Learning
summary: Rehearsal-based continual learning (CL) mitigates catastrophic forgetting by maintaining a subset of samples from previous tasks for replay. Existing studies primarily focus on optimizing memory storage through coreset selection strategies. While these methods are effective, they typically require storing a substantial number of samples per class (SPC), often exceeding 20, to maintain satisfactory performance. In this work, we propose to further compress the memory footprint by synthesizing and storing prototypical exemplars, which can form representative prototypes when passed through the feature extractor. Owing to their representative nature, these exemplars enable the model to retain previous knowledge using only a small number of samples while preserving privacy. Moreover, we introduce a perturbation-based augmentation mechanism that generates synthetic variants of previous data during training, thereby enhancing CL performance. Extensive evaluations on widely used benchmark datasets and settings demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines, particularly in scenarios involving large-scale datasets and a high number of tasks.

AI.tldr: A rehearsal-based continual learning method that uses synthesized prototypical exemplars with perturbation-based augmentation to reduce memory footprint while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Existing rehearsal-based continual learning methods require storing many samples per class (often 20+) to maintain performance. There's a need to compress memory footprint while preserving privacy and performance.
AI.method:
1. Synthesizes and stores prototypical exemplars that form representative prototypes when passed through feature extractor.
2. Uses a perturbation-based augmentation mechanism to generate synthetic variants of previous data during training.
3. Enables retention of previous knowledge with only a small number of samples.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm achieves superior performance compared to existing baselines on widely used benchmark datasets and settings, particularly in scenarios with large-scale datasets and high number of tasks.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully reduces memory footprint in continual learning while maintaining performance through prototypical exemplars and perturbation-based augmentation, offering a more efficient and privacy-preserving approach to rehearsal-based CL.

title: Modeling Matches as Language: A Generative Transformer Approach for Counterfactual Player Valuation in Football
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures, 9 tables. Submitted to 2026 ECML-PKDD Applied Data Science Track
summary: Evaluating football player transfers is challenging because player actions depend strongly on tactical systems, teammates, and match context. Despite this complexity, recruitment decisions often rely on static statistics and subjective expert judgment, which do not fully account for these contextual factors. This limitation stems largely from the absence of counterfactual simulation mechanisms capable of predicting outcomes in hypothetical scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose ScoutGPT, a generative model that treats football match events as sequential tokens within a language modeling framework.
