Utilizing a NanoGPT-based Transformer architecture trained on next-token prediction, ScoutGPT learns the dynamics of match event sequences to simulate event sequences under hypothetical lineups, demonstrating superior predictive performance compared to existing baseline models. Leveraging this capability, the model employs Monte Carlo sampling to enable counterfactual simulation, allowing for the assessment of unobserved scenarios. Experiments on K League data show that simulated player transfers lead to measurable changes in offensive progression and goal probabilities, indicating that ScoutGPT captures player-specific impact beyond traditional static metrics.

AI.tldr: ScoutGPT is a generative model using Transformer architecture to simulate football match event sequences for counterfactual analysis of player transfers, outperforming existing models in predictive performance.
AI.motivation: Football player transfer evaluation is challenging due to dependence on tactical systems, teammates, and match context. Current methods rely on static statistics and subjective expert judgment that don't account for these contextual factors, and lack counterfactual simulation mechanisms for predicting outcomes in hypothetical scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes ScoutGPT, a generative model treating football match events as sequential tokens within a language modeling framework. Uses NanoGPT-based Transformer architecture trained on next-token prediction to learn match event sequence dynamics, enabling simulation of event sequences under hypothetical lineups. Employs Monte Carlo sampling for counterfactual simulation of unobserved scenarios.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior predictive performance compared to existing baseline models. Experiments on K League data show simulated player transfers lead to measurable changes in offensive progression and goal probabilities, indicating ScoutGPT captures player-specific impact beyond traditional static metrics.
AI.conclusion: ScoutGPT successfully addresses the limitations of current football player transfer evaluation methods by providing a counterfactual simulation mechanism that accounts for contextual factors and predicts outcomes in hypothetical scenarios.

title: Collapse or Preserve: Data-Dependent Temporal Aggregation for Spiking Neural Network Acceleration
comment: 15 pages, 8 figures, 7 tables. Code available at https://github.com/jhao/mlx-snn
summary: Spike sparsity is widely believed to enable efficient spiking neural network (SNN) inference on GPU hardware. We demonstrate this is an illusion: five distinct sparse computation strategies on Apple M3 Max all fail to outperform dense convolution, because SIMD architectures cannot exploit the fine-grained, unstructured sparsity of i.i.d. binary spikes. Instead, we propose Temporal Aggregated Convolution (TAC), which exploits convolution linearity to pre-aggregate $K$ spike frames before a single convolution call, reducing $T$ calls to $T/K$. On rate-coded data, TAC achieves 13.8times speedup with +1.6% accuracy on MNIST and +5.4% on Fashion-MNIST -- a simultaneous improvement in both speed and accuracy. However, on event-based data where the temporal dimension carries genuine motion information, TAC's temporal collapse is harmful. We therefore introduce TAC-TP (Temporal Preservation), which shares each group's convolution output across K independent LIF steps, preserving full temporal resolution for downstream layers. On DVS128-Gesture, TAC-TP achieves 95.1% accuracy (vs. 96.3% baseline) with 50% fewer convolution calls, while standard TAC drops to 91.3%. Our key finding is that the optimal temporal aggregation strategy is data-dependent: collapse the temporal dimension for rate-coded data (noise reduction) but preserve it for event data (information retention). Speedup is hardware-agnostic: TAC achieves 11.0times on NVIDIA V100, confirming the mechanism transfers across GPU architectures. All operators in the mlx-snn library are open source.

AI.tldr: Temporal Aggregated Convolution (TAC) exploits convolution linearity to pre-aggregate spike frames for efficient SNN inference, with TAC-TP preserving temporal information for event-based data.
AI.motivation: Contrary to common belief, spike sparsity doesn't enable efficient SNN inference on GPU hardware because SIMD architectures can't exploit the fine-grained, unstructured sparsity of i.i.d. binary spikes.
AI.method: Proposed Temporal Aggregated Convolution (TAC) pre-aggregates K spike frames before a single convolution call (reducing T calls to T/K). TAC-TP variant shares each group's convolution output across K independent LIF steps to preserve temporal resolution for event-based data.
AI.result: On rate-coded data, TAC achieves 13.8× speedup with +1.6% accuracy on MNIST and +5.4% on Fashion-MNIST. On DVS128-Gesture, TAC-TP achieves 95.1% accuracy (vs 96.3% baseline) with 50% fewer convolution calls. The approach works across GPU architectures (11.0× speedup on NVIDIA V100).
AI.conclusion: The optimal temporal aggregation strategy is data-dependent: collapse temporal dimension for rate-coded data (noise reduction) but preserve it for event data (information retention). TAC provides hardware-agnostic speedup across GPU architectures.

title: InterPol: De-anonymizing LM Arena via Interpolated Preference Learning
summary: Strict anonymity of model responses is a key for the reliability of voting-based leaderboards, such as LM Arena. While prior studies have attempted to compromise this assumption using simple statistical features like TF-IDF or bag-ofwords, these methods often lack the discriminative power to distinguish between stylistically similar or within-family models. To overcome these limitations and expose the severity of vulnerability, we introduce INTERPOL, a model-driven identification framework that learns to distinguish target models from others using interpolated preference data. Specifically, INTERPOL captures deep stylistic patterns that superficial statistical features miss by synthesizing hard negative samples through model interpolation and employing an adaptive curriculum learning strategy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that INTERPOL significantly outperforms existing baselines in identification accuracy. Furthermore, we quantify the real-world threat of our findings through ranking manipulation simulations on Arena battle data.

AI.tldr: INTERPOL is a model-driven framework that identifies anonymous AI models in voting-based leaderboards by learning deep stylistic patterns through interpolated preference data and curriculum learning, significantly outperforming existing baselines and demonstrating real-world threats to anonymity.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the vulnerability of voting-based leaderboards (like LM Arena) that rely on strict anonymity of model responses. Prior methods using simple statistical features (TF-IDF, bag-of-words) lack the discriminative power to distinguish between stylistically similar or within-family models, highlighting the need for more sophisticated approaches to expose the severity of these anonymity vulnerabilities.
AI.method: INTERPOL introduces a model-driven identification framework that learns to distinguish target models using interpolated preference data. The method captures deep stylistic patterns that superficial statistical features miss by: 1) synthesizing hard negative samples through model interpolation, and 2) employing an adaptive curriculum learning strategy to effectively learn distinguishing features.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that INTERPOL significantly outperforms existing baselines in identification accuracy. The authors further quantify the real-world threat through ranking manipulation simulations on Arena battle data, showing that their approach can effectively compromise the anonymity assumptions of current leaderboard systems.
AI.conclusion: The INTERPOL framework reveals serious vulnerabilities in voting-based leaderboard systems by showing that strict anonymity can be effectively compromised through sophisticated model-driven identification approaches. This work exposes the need for more robust anonymity protection mechanisms in AI model evaluation platforms and highlights the practical threats to current leaderboard reliability.

title: Effective Sparsity: A Unified Framework via Normalized Entropy and the Effective Number of Nonzeros
summary: Classical sparsity promoting methods rely on the l0 norm, which treats all nonzero components as equally significant. In practical inverse problems, however, solutions often exhibit many small amplitude components that have little effect on reconstruction but lead to an overestimation of signal complexity. We address this limitation by shifting the paradigm from discrete cardinality to effective sparsity. Our approach introduces the effective number of nonzeros (ENZ), a unified class of normalized entropy-based regularizers, including Shannon and Renyi forms, that quantifies the concentration of significant coefficients. We show that, unlike the classical l0 norm, the ENZ provides a stable and continuous measure of effective sparsity that is insensitive to negligible perturbations. For noisy linear inverse problems, we establish theoretical guarantees under the Restricted Isometry Property (RIP), proving that ENZ based recovery is unique and stable. We also derive a decomposition showing that the ENZ equals the support cardinality times a distributional efficiency term, thereby linking entropy with l0 regularization. Numerical experiments show that this effective sparsity framework outperforms traditional cardinality based methods in robustness and accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces effective number of nonzeros (ENZ), a normalized entropy-based regularizer that measures effective sparsity by focusing on significant coefficients rather than all nonzeros, improving robustness and accuracy over traditional l0 methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional l0 norm treats all nonzero components equally, but in practical inverse problems, many small amplitude components have little effect on reconstruction while leading to overestimation of signal complexity. This limitation needs addressing.
AI.method: Proposes effective number of nonzeros (ENZ), a unified class of normalized entropy-based regularizers (including Shannon and Renyi forms) that quantifies concentration of significant coefficients. Provides theoretical guarantees under Restricted Isometry Property (RIP) for noisy linear inverse problems.
AI.result: ENZ provides stable and continuous measure of effective sparsity insensitive to negligible perturbations. Theoretical results show ENZ-based recovery is unique and stable under RIP. ENZ equals support cardinality times distributional efficiency term, linking entropy with l0 regularization. Numerical experiments demonstrate superior robustness and accuracy over traditional cardinality methods.
AI.conclusion: The effective sparsity framework using ENZ regularizers overcomes limitations of classical l0 norm by focusing on significant coefficients rather than all nonzeros, providing better performance in practical inverse problems.

title: SCAN: Sparse Circuit Anchor Interpretable Neuron for Lifelong Knowledge Editing
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often suffer from catastrophic forgetting and collapse during sequential knowledge editing. This vulnerability stems from the prevailing dense editing paradigm, which treats models as black boxes and relies on coarse-grained parameter interventions that inevitably disrupt preserved knowledge. To address this, we propose SCAN (a sparse editing framework based on Sparse Circuit Anchored Neuron) which transforms editing into a mechanism-aware manipulation by constructing a knowledge circuit via Sparse Transcoders. Experiments on Gemma2, Qwen3, and Llama3.1 across CounterFact, ZsRE and WikiFactDiff demonstrate that SCAN achieves a superior performance, maintaining model integrity on benchmarks like MMLU and GSM8K even after 3,000 sequential edits, whereas other existing methods deteriorate progressively as editing accumulates, eventually resulting in model collapse.

AI.tldr: SCAN is a sparse editing framework that uses knowledge circuits to prevent catastrophic forgetting in LLMs during sequential knowledge editing, outperforming existing methods that cause model collapse.
AI.motivation: Current dense editing paradigms treat LLMs as black boxes and use coarse-grained parameter interventions that disrupt preserved knowledge, leading to catastrophic forgetting and model collapse during sequential knowledge editing.
AI.method: SCAN transforms editing into mechanism-aware manipulation by constructing knowledge circuits via Sparse Transcoders, using a sparse editing framework based on Sparse Circuit Anchored Neuron.
AI.result: SCAN achieves superior performance on Gemma2, Qwen3, and Llama3.1 across CounterFact, ZsRE and WikiFactDiff benchmarks, maintaining model integrity on MMLU and GSM8K even after 3,000 sequential edits, while other methods deteriorate progressively and eventually cause model collapse.
AI.conclusion: SCAN's sparse, mechanism-aware approach to knowledge editing effectively prevents catastrophic forgetting and model collapse in LLMs, making it a robust solution for sequential knowledge updates.

title: Exploring the Dimensions of a Variational Neuron
summary:
We introduce EVE (Elemental Variational Expanse), a variational distributional neuron formulated as a local probabilistic computational unit with an explicit prior, an amortized posterior, and unit-level variational regularization. In most modern architectures, uncertainty is modeled through global latent variables or parameter uncertainty, while the computational unit itself remains scalar. EVE instead relocates probabilistic structure to the neuron level, making it locally observable and controllable.
  In this paper, the term dimensions refers primarily to the neuron's internal latent dimensionality, denoted by k. We study how varying k, from the atomic case k = 1 to higher-dimensional latent spaces, changes the neuron's learned operating regime. We then examine how this main axis interacts with two additional structural properties: local capacity control and temporal persistence through a neuron-level autoregressive extension.
  To support this study, EVE is instrumented with internal diagnostics and constraints, including effective KL, a target band on mu^2, out-of-band fractions, and indicators of drift and collapse. Across selected forecasting and tabular settings, we show that latent dimensionality, control, and temporal extension shape the neuron's internal regime, and that some neuron-level variables are measurable, informative, and related to downstream behavior. Overall, the paper provides an experimentally grounded first map of the design space opened by a variational neuron.

AI.tldr: EVE introduces a variational distributional neuron as a local probabilistic computational unit with explicit prior, amortized posterior, and unit-level regularization, enabling neuron-level uncertainty modeling and diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Current neural network architectures model uncertainty through global latent variables or parameter uncertainty, while neurons remain scalar computational units. The authors aim to relocate probabilistic structure to the neuron level, making uncertainty locally observable and controllable.
AI.method: EVE (Elemental Variational Expanse) is proposed as a variational distributional neuron with explicit prior, amortized posterior, and unit-level variational regularization. The paper studies how varying the neuron's internal latent dimensionality (k) affects learning, and examines interactions with local capacity control and temporal persistence via neuron-level autoregressive extension. The system includes internal diagnostics like effective KL, mu² target band, out-of-band fractions, and drift/collapse indicators.
AI.result: Across forecasting and tabular tasks, the study shows that latent dimensionality, capacity control, and temporal extension shape the neuron's internal operating regime. Neuron-level variables are measurable, informative, and relate to downstream behavior.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the first experimentally grounded map of the design space for variational neurons, demonstrating that neuron-level probabilistic structure enables local observability and control of uncertainty, opening new architectural possibilities.

title: Why the Valuable Capabilities of LLMs Are Precisely the Unexplainable Ones
summary: This paper proposes and argues for a counterintuitive thesis: the truly valuable capabilities of large language models (LLMs) reside precisely in the part that cannot be fully captured by human-readable discrete rules. The core argument is a proof by contradiction via expert system equivalence: if the full capabilities of an LLM could be described by a complete set of human-readable rules, then that rule set would be functionally equivalent to an expert system; but expert systems have been historically and empirically demonstrated to be strictly weaker than LLMs; therefore, a contradiction arises -- the capabilities of LLMs that exceed those of expert systems are exactly the capabilities that cannot be rule-encoded. This thesis is further supported by the Chinese philosophical concept of Wu (sudden insight through practice), the historical failure of expert systems, and a structural mismatch between human cognitive tools and complex systems. The paper discusses implications for interpretability research, AI safety, and scientific epistemology.

AI.tldr: LLMs' true value lies in capabilities that cannot be captured by human-readable discrete rules, as proven by contradiction against expert systems equivalence.
AI.motivation: To challenge the assumption that LLM capabilities could be fully captured by explainable rules, and to identify what truly makes LLMs valuable beyond traditional expert systems.
AI.method: Proof by contradiction via expert system equivalence argument: if LLMs could be fully described by human-readable rules, they'd be equivalent to expert systems, but empirical evidence shows LLMs exceed expert systems, thus contradiction exists. Supported by Chinese philosophical concept of Wu, historical expert system failures, and analysis of cognitive tool mismatches.
AI.result: The paper establishes that the valuable capabilities of LLMs reside precisely in the non-rule-encodable aspects, identifying a fundamental limitation of rule-based explanations for understanding LLMs' emergent capabilities.
AI.conclusion: LLMs derive their true power from capabilities that cannot be captured in discrete human-readable rules, with important implications for interpretability research, AI safety approaches, and how we understand scientific knowledge about complex systems.

title: Fronto-parietal and fronto-temporal EEG coherence as predictive neuromarkers of transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation response in treatment-resistant schizophrenia: A machine learning study
comment: This manuscript has been submitted to the Journal of Psychiatric Research. This is a preprint version uploaded to arXiv for open access. The manuscript has not been peer-reviewed or formally published. It contains 34 pages and 3 figures
summary: Response variability limits the clinical utility of transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation (taVNS) for negative symptoms in treatment-resistant schizophrenia (TRS). This study aimed to develop an electroencephalography (EEG)-based machine learning (ML) model to predict individual response and explore associated neurophysiological mechanisms. We used ML to develop and validate predictive models based on pre-treatment EEG data features (power, coherence, and dynamic functional connectivity) from 50 TRS patients enrolled in the taVNS trial, within a nested cross-validation framework. Participants received 20 sessions of active or sham taVNS (n = 25 each) over two weeks, followed by a two-week follow-up. The prediction target was the percentage change in the positive and negative syndrome scale-factor score for negative symptoms (PANSS-FSNS) from baseline to post-treatment, with further evaluation of model specificity and neurophysiological relevance.The optimal model accurately predicted taVNS response in the active group, with predicted PANSS-FSNS changes strongly correlated with observed changes (r = 0.87, p < .001); permutation testing confirmed performance above chance (p < .001). Nine consistently retained features were identified, predominantly fronto-parietal and fronto-temporal coherence features. Negligible predictive performance in the sham group and failure to predict positive symptom change support the predictive specificity of this oscillatory signature for taVNS-related negative symptom improvement. Two coherence features within fronto-parietal-temporal networks showed post-taVNS changes significantly associated with symptom improvement, suggesting dual roles as predictors and potential therapeutic targets. EEG oscillatory neuromarkers enable accurate prediction of individual taVNS response in TRS, supporting mechanism-informed precision neuromodulation strategies.

AI.tldr: EEG-based machine learning model accurately predicts individual response to transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation for negative symptoms in treatment-resistant schizophrenia, identifying fronto-parietal-temporal coherence features as predictive neuromarkers.
AI.motivation: Response variability limits the clinical utility of taVNS for negative symptoms in treatment-resistant schizophrenia, necessitating better prediction of individual treatment response.
AI.method: Developed EEG-based machine learning models using pre-treatment EEG data features (power, coherence, dynamic functional connectivity) from 50 TRS patients in a taVNS trial within a nested cross-validation framework. Participants received 20 sessions of active or sham taVNS over two weeks.
AI.result: Optimal model accurately predicted taVNS response in active group (r = 0.87, p < .001), with nine consistently retained fronto-parietal and fronto-temporal coherence features. Model showed specificity - negligible predictive performance in sham group and failed to predict positive symptom change.
AI.conclusion: EEG oscillatory neuromarkers enable accurate prediction of individual taVNS response in TRS, supporting mechanism-informed precision neuromodulation strategies, with identified coherence features serving dual roles as predictors and potential therapeutic targets.

title: OrigamiBench: An Interactive Environment to Synthesize Flat-Foldable Origamis
summary: Building AI systems that can plan, act, and create in the physical world requires more than pattern recognition. Such systems must understand the causal mechanisms and constraints governing physical processes in order to guide sequential decisions. This capability relies on internal representations, analogous to an internal language model, that relate observations, actions, and resulting environmental changes. However, many existing benchmarks treat visual perception and programmatic reasoning as separate problems, focusing either on visual recognition or on symbolic tasks. The domain of origami provides a natural testbed that integrates these modalities. Constructing shapes through folding operations requires visual perception, reasoning about geometric and physical constraints, and sequential planning, while remaining sufficiently structured for systematic evaluation. We introduce OrigamiBench, an interactive benchmark in which models iteratively propose folds and receive feedback on physical validity and similarity to a target configuration. Experiments with modern vision-language models show that scaling model size alone does not reliably produce causal reasoning about physical transformations. Models fail to generate coherent multi-step folding strategies, suggesting that visual and language representations remain weakly integrated.

AI.tldr: OrigamiBench benchmark shows current vision-language models struggle with causal physical reasoning and multi-step planning for origami folding tasks, despite scaling model size.
AI.motivation: AI systems need causal reasoning about physical mechanisms for planning and acting in the real world, but existing benchmarks separate visual perception from programmatic reasoning, missing integration of these modalities.
AI.method: Created OrigamiBench, an interactive benchmark where models propose folds and receive feedback on physical validity and similarity to targets, using origami as a testbed that integrates visual perception, geometric reasoning, and sequential planning.
AI.result: Modern vision-language models fail to generate coherent multi-step folding strategies; scaling model size alone doesn't produce reliable causal reasoning about physical transformations; visual and language representations remain weakly integrated.
AI.conclusion: Current AI systems lack integrated causal reasoning for physical transformations, suggesting need for new approaches beyond scaling existing architectures to achieve true physical understanding.

title: AGCD: Agent-Guided Cross-Modal Decoding for Weather Forecasting
summary: Accurate weather forecasting is more than grid-wise regression: it must preserve coherent synoptic structures and physical consistency of meteorological fields, especially under autoregressive rollouts where small one-step errors can amplify into structural bias. Existing physics-priors approaches typically impose global, once-for-all constraints via architectures, regularization, or NWP coupling, offering limited state-adaptive and sample-specific controllability at deployment. To bridge this gap, we propose Agent-Guided Cross-modal Decoding (AGCD), a plug-and-play decoding-time prior-injection paradigm that derives state-conditioned physics-priors from the current multivariate atmosphere and injects them into forecasters in a controllable and reusable way. Specifically, We design a multi-agent meteorological narration pipeline to generate state-conditioned physics-priors, utilizing MLLMs to extract various meteorological elements effectively. To effectively apply the priors, AGCD further introduce cross-modal region interaction decoding that performs region-aware multi-scale tokenization and efficient physics-priors injection to refine visual features without changing the backbone interface. Experiments on WeatherBench demonstrate consistent gains for 6-hour forecasting across two resolutions (5.625 degree and 1.40625 degree) and diverse backbones (generic and weather-specialized), including strictly causal 48-hour autoregressive rollouts that reduce early-stage error accumulation and improve long-horizon stability.

AI.tldr: AGCD introduces a plug-and-play decoding-time method that uses multi-agent MLLMs to extract state-conditioned physics priors from current atmospheric data and injects them into weather forecasting models through cross-modal region interaction decoding, improving forecast accuracy especially in autoregressive rollouts.
AI.motivation: Current physics-priors approaches in weather forecasting impose global constraints that lack state-adaptive and sample-specific controllability during deployment. These methods typically use fixed architectures, regularization, or NWP coupling that don't adapt well to specific atmospheric conditions, limiting their effectiveness in preventing error accumulation during autoregressive forecasting rollouts.
AI.method: AGCD uses a multi-agent meteorological narration pipeline with MLLMs to extract state-conditioned physics priors from current multivariate atmosphere data. It then employs cross-modal region interaction decoding with region-aware multi-scale tokenization to inject these priors into forecasters in a controllable way without changing the backbone interface.
AI.result: Experiments on WeatherBench show consistent improvements for 6-hour forecasting across two resolutions (5.625° and 1.40625°) and diverse backbones, including strict 48-hour autoregressive rollouts that reduce early-stage error accumulation and improve long-horizon stability.
AI.conclusion: AGCD provides an effective plug-and-play solution for injecting state-adaptive physics priors into weather forecasting models, addressing limitations of current approaches by enabling sample-specific controllability and improving forecast accuracy, particularly in challenging autoregressive scenarios where error amplification is a concern.

title: On Interpolation Formulas Describing Neural Network Generalization
summary: In 2020 Domingos introduced an interpolation formula valid for "every model trained by gradient descent". He concluded that such models behave approximately as kernel machines. In this work, we extend the Domingos formula to stochastic training. We introduce a stochastic gradient kernel that extends the deterministic version via a continuous-time diffusion approximation. We prove stochastic Domingos theorems and show that the expected network output admits a kernel-machine representation with optimizer-specific weighting. It reveals that training samples contribute through loss-dependent weights and gradient alignment along the training trajectory. We then link the generalization error to the null space of the integral operator induced by the stochastic gradient kernel. The same path-kernel viewpoint provides a unified interpretation of diffusion models and GANs: diffusion induces stage-wise, noise-localized corrections, whereas GANs induce distribution-guided corrections shaped by discriminator geometry. We visualize the evolution of implicit kernels during optimization and quantify out-of-distribution behaviors through a series of numerical experiments. Our results support a feature-space memory view of learning: training stores data-dependent information in an evolving tangent feature geometry, and predictions at test time arise from kernel-weighted retrieval and aggregation of these stored features, with generalization governed by alignment between test points and the learned feature memory.

AI.tldr: The paper extends Domingos' interpolation formula to stochastic training, introducing a stochastic gradient kernel that reveals training samples contribute through loss-dependent weights and gradient alignment, with implications for understanding diffusion models and GANs through a unified kernel perspective.
AI.motivation: To extend Domingos' deterministic interpolation formula (which shows gradient-descent trained models behave as kernel machines) to the more practical case of stochastic training, and to develop a unified theoretical framework for understanding how neural networks store and retrieve information during learning.
AI.method: Introduces a stochastic gradient kernel via continuous-time diffusion approximation, proves stochastic Domingos theorems showing expected network output has kernel-machine representation with optimizer-specific weighting, links generalization error to null space of integral operator from this kernel, applies path-kernel viewpoint to interpret diffusion models and GANs, and conducts numerical experiments to visualize kernel evolution and quantify out-of-distribution behavior.
AI.result: Shows that stochastic training leads to kernel-machine behavior with loss-dependent weights and gradient alignment contributions, reveals diffusion models use stage-wise noise-localized corrections while GANs use distribution-guided corrections shaped by discriminator geometry, and supports a feature-space memory view where training stores data-dependent information in evolving tangent feature geometry.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified kernel perspective for understanding stochastic training, showing neural networks can be viewed as kernel machines with training-trajectory-dependent weighting, offering insights into generalization behavior and connecting seemingly different models like diffusion models and GANs through the same theoretical framework.

title: Probe-then-Plan: Environment-Aware Planning for Industrial E-commerce Search
summary: Modern e-commerce search is evolving to resolve complex user intents. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer strong reasoning, existing LLM-based paradigms face a fundamental blindness-latency dilemma: query rewriting is agnostic to retrieval capabilities and real-time inventory, yielding invalid plans; conversely, deep search agents rely on iterative tool calls and reflection, incurring seconds of latency incompatible with industrial sub-second budgets. To resolve this conflict, we propose Environment-Aware Search Planning (EASP), reformulating search planning as a dynamic reasoning process grounded in environmental reality. EASP introduces a Probe-then-Plan mechanism: a lightweight Retrieval Probe exposes the retrieval snapshot, enabling the Planner to diagnose execution gaps and generate grounded search plans. The methodology comprises three stages: (1) Offline Data Synthesis: A Teacher Agent synthesizes diverse, execution-validated plans by diagnosing the probed environment. (2) Planner Training and Alignment: The Planner is initialized via Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) to internalize diagnostic capabilities, then aligned with business outcomes (conversion rate) via Reinforcement Learning (RL). (3) Adaptive Online Serving: A complexity-aware routing mechanism selectively activates planning for complex queries, ensuring optimal resource allocation. Extensive offline evaluations and online A/B testing on JD.com demonstrate that EASP significantly improves relevant recall and achieves substantial lifts in UCVR and GMV. EASP has been successfully deployed in JD.com's AI-Search system.

AI.tldr: EASP is an environment-aware search planning framework that resolves the blindness-latency dilemma in e-commerce search by introducing a Probe-then-Plan mechanism, enabling grounded search planning through real-time inventory awareness while maintaining sub-second latency.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based search approaches face a fundamental blindness-latency dilemma: query rewriting methods are blind to retrieval capabilities and real-time inventory, producing invalid plans, while deep search agents are too slow (seconds of latency) for industrial sub-second response budgets.
AI.method: Propose Environment-Aware Search Planning (EASP) with Probe-then-Plan mechanism. Approach has three stages: 1) Offline Data Synthesis using a Teacher Agent to create execution-validated plans; 2) Planner Training via SFT then RL alignment with business outcomes; 3) Adaptive Online Serving with complexity-aware routing for selective activation.
AI.result: Extensive offline evaluations and online A/B testing on JD.com show significant improvements in relevant recall and substantial lifts in UCVR (User Conversion Rate) and GMV (Gross Merchandise Volume). Successfully deployed in JD.com's AI-Search system.
AI.conclusion: EASP effectively resolves the blindness-latency dilemma in e-commerce search by grounding LLM reasoning in environmental reality through the Probe-then-Plan mechanism, achieving both execution validity and sub-second latency, making it practical for industrial deployment.

title: Scribe Verification in Chinese manuscripts using Siamese, Triplet, and Vision Transformer Neural Networks
comment: Proceedings DBKDA 2026, The Eighteenth International Conference on Advances in Databases, Knowledge, and Data Applications, Valencia, Spain, March 10-11, 2026
summary: The paper examines deep learning models for scribe verification in Chinese manuscripts. That is, to automatically determine whether two manuscript fragments were written by the same scribe using deep metric learning methods. Two datasets were used: the Tsinghua Bamboo Slips Dataset and a selected subset of the Multi-Attribute Chinese Calligraphy Dataset, focusing on the calligraphers with a large number of samples. Siamese and Triplet neural network architectures are implemented, including convolutional and Transformer-based models. The experimental results show that the MobileNetV3+ Custom Siamese model trained with contrastive loss achieves either the best or the second-best overall accuracy and area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve on both datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper applies deep metric learning with Siamese and Triplet networks for scribe verification in Chinese manuscripts, achieving state-of-the-art results using MobileNetV3+ Custom Siamese with contrastive loss.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to automate the process of determining whether two manuscript fragments were written by the same scribe, which is important for historical document analysis, paleography, and manuscript authentication. Traditional manual verification is time-consuming and subjective, creating a need for automated, objective methods using deep learning.
AI.method: The research uses deep metric learning approaches with two types of neural network architectures: Siamese and Triplet networks. Both convolutional (like MobileNetV3) and Transformer-based models are implemented. They evaluate these methods on two datasets: Tsinghua Bamboo Slips Dataset and a curated subset of the Multi-Attribute Chinese Calligraphy Dataset containing calligraphers with sufficient samples.
AI.result: The MobileNetV3+ Custom Siamese model trained with contrastive loss achieves either the best or second-best performance on both datasets, measured by overall accuracy and area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve. This indicates that contrastive learning with customized Siamese architectures is particularly effective for scribe verification tasks.
AI.conclusion: Deep metric learning methods, particularly Siamese networks with contrastive loss, are highly effective for automated scribe verification in Chinese manuscripts. The MobileNetV3-based approach demonstrates superior performance, offering a robust solution for authenticating manuscript authorship and advancing the field of digital paleography.

title: Advancing Multimodal Agent Reasoning with Long-Term Neuro-Symbolic Memory
summary: Recent advances in large language models have driven the emergence of intelligent agents operating in open-world, multimodal environments. To support long-term reasoning, such agents are typically equipped with external memory systems. However, most existing multimodal agent memories rely primarily on neural representations and vector-based retrieval, which are well-suited for inductive, intuitive reasoning but fundamentally limited in supporting analytical, deductive reasoning critical for real-world decision making. To address this limitation, we propose NS-Mem, a long-term neuro-symbolic memory framework designed to advance multimodal agent reasoning by integrating neural memory with explicit symbolic structures and rules. Specifically, NS-Mem is operated around three core components of a memory system: (1) a three-layer memory architecture that consists episodic layer, semantic layer and logic rule layer, (2) a memory construction and maintenance mechanism implemented by SK-Gen that automatically consolidates structured knowledge from accumulated multimodal experiences and incrementally updates both neural representations and symbolic rules, and (3) a hybrid memory retrieval mechanism that combines similarity-based search with deterministic symbolic query functions to support structured reasoning. Experiments on real-world multimodal reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that Neural-Symbolic Memory achieves an average 4.35% improvement in overall reasoning accuracy over pure neural memory systems, with gains of up to 12.5% on constrained reasoning queries, validating the effectiveness of NS-Mem.

AI.tldr: NS-Mem: A neuro-symbolic memory framework for multimodal agents integrating neural representations with symbolic structures to enhance analytical reasoning
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal agent memories rely primarily on neural representations and vector-based retrieval, which are limited for analytical, deductive reasoning needed for real-world decision making
AI.method: Three-layer memory architecture (episodic, semantic, logic rule layers), memory construction via SK-Gen that consolidates structured knowledge, and hybrid retrieval combining similarity search with symbolic query functions
AI.result: Average 4.35% improvement in overall reasoning accuracy over pure neural memory systems, with gains up to 12.5% on constrained reasoning queries
AI.conclusion: NS-Mem effectively enhances multimodal agent reasoning by integrating neural memory with symbolic structures and rules, validated by benchmark experiments

title: UVLM: A Universal Vision-Language Model Loader for Reproducible Multimodal Benchmarking
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for image understanding tasks, yet their practical deployment remains hindered by significant architectural heterogeneity across model families. This paper introduces UVLM (Universal Vision-Language Model Loader), a Google Colab-based framework that provides a unified interface for loading, configuring, and benchmarking multiple VLM architectures on custom image analysis tasks. UVLM currently supports two major model families -- LLaVA-NeXT and Qwen2.5-VL -- which differ fundamentally in their vision encoding, tokenization, and decoding strategies. The framework abstracts these differences behind a single inference function, enabling researchers to compare models using identical prompts and evaluation protocols. Key features include a multi-task prompt builder with support for four response types (numeric, category, boolean, text), a consensus validation mechanism based on majority voting across repeated inferences, a flexible token budget (up to 1,500 tokens) enabling users to design custom reasoning strategies through prompt engineering, and a built-in chain-of-thought reference mode for benchmarking. UVLM is designed for reproducibility, accessibility, and extensibility and as such is freely deployable on Google Colab using consumer-grade GPU resources. The paper also presents the first benchmarking of different VLMs on tasks of increasing reasoning complexity using a corpus of 120 street-view images.

AI.tldr: UVLM is a Google Colab framework that provides a unified interface for loading, configuring, and benchmarking heterogeneous Vision-Language Models (LLaVA-NeXT and Qwen2.5-VL) on custom image analysis tasks.
AI.motivation: VLMs have emerged as powerful tools for image understanding, but their practical deployment is hindered by significant architectural heterogeneity across model families, making comparison and benchmarking difficult.
AI.method: Developed UVLM, a Google Colab-based framework that abstracts architectural differences behind a single inference function. It includes multi-task prompt builder (numeric, category, boolean, text outputs), consensus validation via majority voting, flexible token budget (up to 1,500 tokens), built-in chain-of-thought reference mode, and supports reproducibility on consumer-grade GPUs.
AI.result: Successfully benchmarked different VLMs on tasks of increasing reasoning complexity using a corpus of 120 street-view images. The framework enables direct comparison of models using identical prompts and evaluation protocols.
AI.conclusion: UVLM provides an accessible, reproducible, and extensible solution for VLM benchmarking that addresses architectural heterogeneity, making VLM comparison and deployment more practical for researchers.

title: Algorithms for Deciding the Safety of States in Fully Observable Non-deterministic Problems: Technical Report
summary: Learned action policies are increasingly popular in sequential decision-making, but suffer from a lack of safety guarantees. Recent work introduced a pipeline for testing the safety of such policies under initial-state and action-outcome non-determinism. At the pipeline's core, is the problem of deciding whether a state is safe (a safe policy exists from the state) and finding faults, which are state-action pairs that transition from a safe state to an unsafe one. Their most effective algorithm for deciding safety, TarjanSafe, is effective on their benchmarks, but we show that it has exponential worst-case runtime with respect to the state space. A linear-time alternative exists, but it is slower in practice. We close this gap with a new policy-iteration algorithm iPI, that combines the best of both: it matches TarjanSafe's best-case runtime while guaranteeing a polynomial worst-case. Experiments confirm our theory and show that in problems amenable to TarjanSafe iPI has similar performance, whereas in ill-suited problems iPI scales exponentially better.

AI.tldr: A new polynomial-time algorithm (iPI) for verifying safety of learned action policies that combines the strengths of existing exponential (TarjanSafe) and linear-time methods
AI.motivation: Learned action policies lack safety guarantees, and existing verification methods either have exponential worst-case runtime (TarjanSafe) or are too slow in practice despite having polynomial guarantees
AI.method: Developed a new policy-iteration algorithm called iPI that combines the best of TarjanSafe's efficiency in practice with polynomial worst-case complexity guarantees
AI.result: iPI matches TarjanSafe's performance on amenable problems while scaling exponentially better on ill-suited problems, with both theoretical polynomial guarantees and practical effectiveness
AI.conclusion: The iPI algorithm successfully bridges the gap between theoretical guarantees and practical performance for safety verification of learned policies, offering polynomial worst-case complexity while maintaining competitive practical efficiency

title: Robust Self-Training with Closed-loop Label Correction for Learning from Noisy Labels
summary: Training deep neural networks with noisy labels remains a significant challenge, often leading to degraded performance. Existing methods for handling label noise typically rely on either transition matrix, noise detection, or meta-learning techniques, but they often exhibit low utilization efficiency of noisy samples and incur high computational costs. In this paper, we propose a self-training label correction framework using decoupled bilevel optimization, where a classifier and neural correction function co-evolve. Leveraging a small clean dataset, our method employs noisy posterior simulation and intermediate features to transfer ground-truth knowledge, forming a closed-loop feedback system that prevents error amplification. Theoretical guarantees underpin the stability of our approach, and extensive experiments on benchmark datasets like CIFAR and Clothing1M confirm state-of-the-art performance with reduced training time, highlighting its practical applicability for learning from noisy labels.

AI.tldr: A self-training label correction framework using decoupled bilevel optimization where a classifier and neural correction function co-evolve, achieving state-of-the-art performance on noisy label datasets with reduced training time.
AI.motivation: Training deep neural networks with noisy labels degrades performance. Existing methods (transition matrix, noise detection, meta-learning) suffer from low utilization efficiency of noisy samples and high computational costs.
AI.method: Proposes a self-training label correction framework with decoupled bilevel optimization, where classifier and neural correction function co-evolve. Uses noisy posterior simulation and intermediate features from a small clean dataset to transfer ground-truth knowledge, forming a closed-loop feedback system that prevents error amplification.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees underpin stability. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets (CIFAR, Clothing1M) confirm state-of-the-art performance with reduced training time, highlighting practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses noisy label learning challenges by combining self-training with decoupled bilevel optimization, achieving superior performance while being computationally efficient and theoretically sound.

title: Evolutionary Transfer Learning for Dragonchess
summary: Dragonchess, a three-dimensional chess variant introduced by Gary Gygax, presents unique strategic and computational challenges that make it an ideal environment for studying the transfer of artificial intelligence (AI) heuristics across domains. In this work, we introduce Dragonchess as a novel testbed for AI research and provide an open-source, Python-based game engine for community use. Our research investigates evolutionary transfer learning by adapting heuristic evaluation functions directly from Stockfish, a leading chess engine, and subsequently optimizing them using Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES). Initial trials showed that direct heuristic transfers were inadequate due to Dragonchess's distinct multi-layer structure and movement rules. However, evolutionary optimization significantly improved AI agent performance, resulting in superior gameplay demonstrated through empirical evaluation in a 50-round Swiss-style tournament. This research establishes the effectiveness of evolutionary methods in adapting heuristic knowledge to structurally complex, previously unexplored game domains.

AI.tldr: Researchers adapt Stockfish chess heuristics via evolutionary optimization to create AI for Dragonchess, a 3D chess variant, demonstrating successful knowledge transfer to complex new domains.
AI.motivation: Dragonchess presents unique strategic and computational challenges that make it an ideal environment for studying AI heuristic transfer across domains. The paper aims to establish it as a novel testbed for AI research.
AI.method: Created an open-source Python game engine for Dragonchess, adapted heuristic evaluation functions from Stockfish, and optimized them using Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES). Evaluated performance in a 50-round Swiss-style tournament.
AI.result: Direct heuristic transfers from Stockfish were inadequate due to Dragonchess's distinct multi-layer structure and movement rules. However, evolutionary optimization significantly improved AI agent performance, resulting in superior gameplay.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary methods are effective for adapting heuristic knowledge to structurally complex, previously unexplored game domains, establishing Dragonchess as a valuable testbed for AI research.

title: Distributed Acoustic Sensing for Urban Traffic Monitoring: Spatio-Temporal Attention in Recurrent Neural Networks
summary: Effective urban traffic monitoring is essential for improving mobility, enhancing safety, and supporting sustainable cities. Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) enables large-scale traffic observation by transforming existing fiber-optic infrastructure into dense arrays of vibration sensors. However, modeling the high-resolution spatio-temporal structure of DAS data for reliable traffic event recognition remains challenging. This study presents a real-world DAS-based traffic monitoring experiment conducted in Granada, Spain, where vehicles cross a fiber deployed perpendicular to the roadway. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are employed to model intra- and inter-event temporal dependencies. Spatial and temporal attention mechanisms are systematically integrated within the RNN architecture to analyze their impact on recognition performance, parameter efficiency, and interpretability. Results show that an appropriate and complementary placement of attention modules improves the balance between accuracy and model complexity. Attention heatmaps provide physically meaningful interpretations of classification decisions by highlighting informative spatial locations and temporal segments. Furthermore, the proposed SA-bi-TA configuration demonstrates spatial transferability, successfully recognizing traffic events at sensing locations different from those used during training, with only moderate performance degradation. These findings support the development of scalable and interpretable DAS-based traffic monitoring systems capable of operating under heterogeneous urban sensing conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a DAS-based traffic monitoring system using RNNs with spatial and temporal attention mechanisms to improve vehicle event recognition, achieving better accuracy-complexity balance and interpretability while demonstrating spatial transferability.
AI.motivation: Effective urban traffic monitoring is crucial for mobility, safety, and sustainability. While DAS enables large-scale traffic observation using existing fiber-optic infrastructure, modeling the high-resolution spatio-temporal structure of DAS data for reliable traffic event recognition remains challenging.
AI.method: The study conducted a real-world DAS-based traffic monitoring experiment in Granada, Spain, with vehicles crossing a fiber deployed perpendicular to the roadway. They employed recurrent neural networks (RNNs) to model intra- and inter-event temporal dependencies, and systematically integrated spatial and temporal attention mechanisms within the RNN architecture.
AI.result: Results show that appropriate placement of attention modules improves the balance between accuracy and model complexity. Attention heatmaps provide physically meaningful interpretations of classification decisions. The proposed SA-bi-TA configuration demonstrates spatial transferability, successfully recognizing traffic events at different sensing locations than those used during training, with only moderate performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: These findings support the development of scalable and interpretable DAS-based traffic monitoring systems capable of operating under heterogeneous urban sensing conditions, advancing the practical application of DAS technology for urban traffic management.

title: FedPBS: Proximal-Balanced Scaling Federated Learning Model for Robust Personalized Training for Non-IID Data
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables a set of distributed clients to jointly train machine learning models while preserving their local data privacy, making it attractive for applications in healthcare, finance, mobility, and smart-city systems. However, FL faces several challenges, including statistical heterogeneity and uneven client participation, which can degrade convergence and model quality. In this work, we propose FedPBS, an FL algorithm that couples complementary ideas from FedBS and FedProx to address these challenges. FedPBS dynamically adapts batch sizes to client resources to support balanced and scalable participation, and selectively applies a proximal correction to small-batch clients to stabilize local updates and reduce divergence from the global model. Experiments on benchmarking datasets such as CIFAR-10 and UCI-HAR under highly non-IID settings demonstrate that FedPBS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, including FedBS, FedGA, MOON, and FedProx. The results demonstrate robust performance gains under extreme data heterogeneity, with smooth loss curves indicating stable convergence across diverse federated environments. FedPBS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art federated learning baselines on UCI-HAR and CIFAR-10 under severe non-IID conditions while maintaining stable and reliable convergence.

AI.tldr: FedPBS combines FedBS and FedProx concepts to handle statistical heterogeneity and uneven client participation in federated learning by dynamically adapting batch sizes and applying proximal corrections.
AI.motivation: FL faces challenges including statistical heterogeneity and uneven client participation that degrade convergence and model quality. These issues are particularly problematic in real-world applications like healthcare, finance, mobility, and smart-city systems.
AI.method: FedPBS couples complementary ideas from FedBS and FedProx: 1) Dynamically adapts batch sizes to client resources to support balanced and scalable participation, 2) Selectively applies proximal correction to small-batch clients to stabilize local updates and reduce divergence from global model.
AI.result: FedPBS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods (FedBS, FedGA, MOON, FedProx) on benchmarking datasets (CIFAR-10, UCI-HAR) under highly non-IID settings. Shows robust performance gains under extreme data heterogeneity with smooth loss curves indicating stable convergence across diverse federated environments.
AI.conclusion: FedPBS effectively addresses FL challenges of statistical heterogeneity and uneven client participation by combining batch size adaptation with selective proximal correction, resulting in superior performance and stable convergence in non-IID federated environments.

title: Close to Reality: Interpretable and Feasible Data Augmentation for Imbalanced Learning
summary:
Many machine learning classification tasks involve imbalanced datasets, which are often subject to over-sampling techniques aimed at improving model performance. However, these techniques are prone to generating unrealistic or infeasible samples. Furthermore, they often function as black boxes, lacking interpretability in their procedures. This opacity makes it difficult to track their effectiveness and provide necessary adjustments, and they may ultimately fail to yield significant performance improvements.
  To bridge this gap, we introduce the Decision Predicate Graphs for Data Augmentation (DPG-da), a framework that extracts interpretable decision predicates from trained models to capture domain rules and enforce them during sample generation. This design ensures that over-sampled data remain diverse, constraint-satisfying, and interpretable. In experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmark datasets, DPG-da consistently improves classification performance over traditional over-sampling methods, while guaranteeing logical validity and offering clear, interpretable explanations of the over-sampled data.

AI.tldr: DPG-da is an interpretable data augmentation framework that extracts decision predicates from trained models to generate constraint-satisfying synthetic samples for imbalanced datasets, improving classification performance while ensuring logical validity.
AI.motivation: Traditional over-sampling techniques for imbalanced datasets often generate unrealistic samples, lack interpretability, and may not significantly improve performance, creating a need for more transparent and effective methods.
AI.method: DPG-da extracts interpretable decision predicates from trained models to capture domain rules, then enforces these constraints during sample generation to ensure synthetic data remains diverse, valid, and interpretable.
AI.result: DPG-da consistently outperforms traditional over-sampling methods on both synthetic and real-world benchmark datasets while guaranteeing logical validity and providing clear explanations for generated samples.
AI.conclusion: DPG-da effectively bridges the gap in interpretable data augmentation for imbalanced datasets, offering both performance improvements and transparency over traditional black-box over-sampling methods.

title: CRASH: Cognitive Reasoning Agent for Safety Hazards in Autonomous Driving
summary: As AVs grow in complexity and diversity, identifying the root causes of operational failures has become increasingly complex. The heterogeneity of system architectures across manufacturers, ranging from end-to-end to modular designs, together with variations in algorithms and integration strategies, limits the standardization of incident investigations and hinders systematic safety analysis. This work examines real-world AV incidents reported in the NHTSA database. We curate a dataset of 2,168 cases reported between 2021 and 2025, representing more than 80 million miles driven. To process this data, we introduce CRASH, Cognitive Reasoning Agent for Safety Hazards, an LLM-based agent that automates reasoning over crash reports by leveraging both standardized fields and unstructured narrative descriptions. CRASH operates on a unified representation of each incident to generate concise summaries, attribute a primary cause, and assess whether the AV materially contributed to the event. Our findings show that (1) CRASH attributes 64% of incidents to perception or planning failures, underscoring the importance of reasoning-based analysis for accurate fault attribution; and (2) approximately 50% of reported incidents involve rear-end collisions, highlighting a persistent and unresolved challenge in autonomous driving deployment. We further validate CRASH with five domain experts, achieving 86% accuracy in attributing AV system failures. Overall, CRASH demonstrates strong potential as a scalable and interpretable tool for automated crash analysis, providing actionable insights to support safety research and the continued development of autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: LLM-based agent CRASH automates analysis of AV crash reports, identifying perception/planning failures as primary causes in 64% of incidents and revealing 50% are rear-end collisions.
AI.motivation: The heterogeneity of AV system architectures across manufacturers (end-to-end vs modular designs) with varying algorithms and integration strategies makes incident investigations complex and limits standardization, hindering systematic safety analysis of AV failures.
AI.method: The authors introduce CRASH (Cognitive Reasoning Agent for Safety Hazards), an LLM-based agent that processes 2,168 real-world AV incidents from NHTSA database (2021-2025, 80+ million miles). CRASH uses a unified representation of incidents to analyze both standardized fields and unstructured narrative descriptions, generating summaries, attributing primary causes, and assessing AV contribution.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) CRASH attributes 64% of incidents to perception or planning failures; (2) Approximately 50% of reported incidents involve rear-end collisions. CRASH achieves 86% accuracy in attributing AV system failures when validated with five domain experts.
AI.conclusion: CRASH demonstrates strong potential as a scalable and interpretable tool for automated crash analysis, providing actionable insights to support AV safety research and development by enabling systematic analysis of heterogeneous AV incident data.

title: True 4-Bit Quantized Convolutional Neural Network Training on CPU: Achieving Full-Precision Parity
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures, 9 tables. Code available at https://github.com/shivnathtathe/vgg4bit-and-simpleresnet4bit
summary: Low-precision neural network training has emerged as a promising direction for reducing computational costs and democratizing access to deep learning research. However, existing 4-bit quantization methods either rely on expensive GPU infrastructure or suffer from significant accuracy degradation. In this work, we present a practical method for training convolutional neural networks at true 4-bit precision using standard PyTorch operations on commodity CPUs. We introduce a novel tanh-based soft weight clipping technique that, combined with symmetric quantization, dynamic per-layer scaling, and straight-through estimators, achieves stable convergence and competitive accuracy. Training a VGG-style architecture with 3.25 million parameters from scratch on CIFAR-10, our method achieves 92.34% test accuracy on Google Colab's free CPU tier -- matching full-precision baseline performance (92.5%) with only a 0.16% gap. We further validate on CIFAR-100, achieving 70.94% test accuracy across 100 classes with the same architecture and training procedure, demonstrating that 4-bit training from scratch generalizes to harder classification tasks. Both experiments achieve 8x memory compression over FP32 while maintaining exactly 15 unique weight values per layer throughout training. We additionally validate hardware independence by demonstrating rapid convergence on a consumer mobile device (OnePlus 9R), achieving 83.16% accuracy in only 6 epochs. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has demonstrated 4-bit quantization-aware training achieving full-precision parity on standard CPU hardware without specialized kernels or post-training quantization.

AI.tldr: A practical method for training convolutional neural networks at true 4-bit precision using standard PyTorch on commodity CPUs, achieving near-full-precision accuracy with 8x memory compression.
AI.motivation: Existing 4-bit quantization methods either require expensive GPU infrastructure or suffer from significant accuracy degradation, limiting accessibility to deep learning research. There's a need for practical low-precision training methods that work on standard hardware without specialized kernels.
AI.method: The method uses a novel tanh-based soft weight clipping technique combined with symmetric quantization, dynamic per-layer scaling, and straight-through estimators. It trains convolutional neural networks at true 4-bit precision using standard PyTorch operations on commodity CPUs, maintaining exactly 15 unique weight values per layer throughout training.
AI.result: Achieved 92.34% test accuracy on CIFAR-10 with a VGG-style architecture, matching full-precision baseline (92.5%) with only 0.16% gap. On CIFAR-100, achieved 70.94% test accuracy. Achieved 8x memory compression over FP32 while maintaining convergence. Also validated on consumer mobile device (OnePlus 9R), achieving 83.16% accuracy in only 6 epochs.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that 4-bit quantization-aware training can achieve full-precision parity on standard CPU hardware without specialized kernels or post-training quantization, making low-precision neural network training practical and accessible on commodity hardware.

title: Brain-Inspired Graph Multi-Agent Systems for LLM Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across a wide range of language tasks, yet complex multi-step reasoning remains a fundamental challenge. While Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) equipped with extended chain-of-thought mechanisms demonstrate improved performance over standard LLMs, both model types still suffer from accuracy collapse on sufficiently complex tasks, suggesting that scaling model-level reasoning alone is insufficient. Inspired by the global workspace theory of human cognition, we propose Brain-Inspired Graph Multi-Agent Systems (BIGMAS), in which specialized LLM agents are organized as nodes in a dynamically constructed directed graph and coordinate exclusively through a centralized shared workspace. A problem-adaptive GraphDesigner constructs task-specific agent topologies, while a global Orchestrator leverages the complete shared state for routing decisions, overcoming the local-view bottleneck of reactive approaches. Experiments on Game24, Six Fives, and Tower of London across six frontier LLMs demonstrate that BIGMAS consistently improves reasoning performance for both standard LLMs and LRMs, outperforming existing multi-agent baselines including ReAct and Tree of Thoughts, showing that multi-agent architectural design provides complementary gains orthogonal to model-level reasoning enhancements.

AI.tldr: BIGMAS is a brain-inspired multi-agent system where specialized LLM agents organized in dynamic graphs coordinate through a shared workspace, significantly improving reasoning performance on complex tasks beyond what scaling model-level reasoning alone can achieve.
AI.motivation: Standard LLMs and even enhanced Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) with chain-of-thought mechanisms still suffer from accuracy collapse on sufficiently complex multi-step reasoning tasks, suggesting that simply scaling up model-level reasoning is insufficient.
AI.method: BIGMAS organizes specialized LLM agents as nodes in dynamically constructed directed graphs, with coordination through a centralized shared workspace. A GraphDesigner constructs task-specific agent topologies, and a global Orchestrator makes routing decisions using the complete shared state to overcome local-view limitations.
AI.result: Experiments on Game24, Six Fives, and Tower of London across six frontier LLMs show BIGMAS consistently improves reasoning performance for both standard LLMs and LRMs, outperforming existing multi-agent baselines like ReAct and Tree of Thoughts.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent architectural design provides complementary gains orthogonal to model-level reasoning enhancements, demonstrating that organizing specialized agents in brain-inspired graph structures with shared workspace coordination can overcome limitations of current reasoning approaches.

title: Shapes are not enough: CONSERVAttack and its use for finding vulnerabilities and uncertainties in machine learning applications
summary: In High Energy Physics, as in many other fields of science, the application of machine learning techniques has been crucial in advancing our understanding of fundamental phenomena. Increasingly, deep learning models are applied to analyze both simulated and experimental data. In most experiments, a rigorous regime of testing for physically motivated systematic uncertainties is in place. The numerical evaluation of these tests for differences between the data on the one side and simulations on the other side quantifies the effect of potential sources of mismodelling on the machine learning output. In addition, thorough comparisons of marginal distributions and (linear) feature correlations between data and simulation in "control regions" are applied. However, the guidance by physical motivation, and the need to constrain comparisons to specific regions, does not guarantee that all possible sources of deviations have been accounted for. We therefore propose a new adversarial attack - the CONSERVAttack - designed to exploit the remaining space of hypothetical deviations between simulation and data after the above mentioned tests. The resulting adversarial perturbations are consistent within the uncertainty bounds - evading standard validation checks - while successfully fooling the underlying model. We further propose strategies to mitigate such vulnerabilities and argue that robustness to adversarial effects must be considered when interpreting results from deep learning in particle physics.

AI.tldr: A novel adversarial attack called CONSERVAttack is proposed to exploit remaining vulnerabilities in particle physics ML models after standard validation tests, with mitigation strategies suggested to improve robustness.
AI.motivation: Current ML validation in high energy physics relies on physically motivated systematic uncertainty tests and control region comparisons, but these may not account for all possible deviations between simulation and experimental data, leaving models vulnerable to unconsidered differences.
AI.method: The authors develop CONSERVAttack, an adversarial attack designed to exploit the remaining space of hypothetical deviations between simulation and data that evade standard validation checks, while staying within uncertainty bounds to avoid detection.
AI.result: The CONSERVAttack successfully generates adversarial perturbations that fool underlying ML models while being consistent with uncertainty bounds, demonstrating that current validation approaches are insufficient to guarantee model robustness.
AI.conclusion: Robustness to adversarial effects must be considered when interpreting results from deep learning in particle physics, and the proposed attack highlights vulnerabilities that need to be addressed through improved validation strategies and mitigation approaches.

title: Why AI systems don't learn and what to do about it: Lessons on autonomous learning from cognitive science
summary: We critically examine the limitations of current AI models in achieving autonomous learning and propose a learning architecture inspired by human and animal cognition. The proposed framework integrates learning from observation (System A) and learning from active behavior (System B) while flexibly switching between these learning modes as a function of internally generated meta-control signals (System M). We discuss how this could be built by taking inspiration on how organisms adapt to real-world, dynamic environments across evolutionary and developmental timescales.

AI.tldr: Proposes a cognitive-inspired AI architecture combining observational and behavioral learning with meta-control switching, drawing from biological adaptation principles.
AI.motivation: Current AI models have limitations in achieving true autonomous learning. The paper aims to address these limitations by drawing inspiration from how humans and animals learn and adapt to dynamic environments across different timescales.
AI.method: Proposes a three-system framework: System A (learning from observation), System B (learning from active behavior), and System M (meta-control system that flexibly switches between learning modes based on internally generated signals). The architecture is inspired by biological cognition and evolutionary/developmental adaptation.
AI.result: The paper presents a theoretical framework that integrates different learning modalities with adaptive control mechanisms. It discusses how this architecture could be built by taking inspiration from biological organisms' adaptation strategies.
AI.conclusion: A biologically-inspired cognitive architecture combining multiple learning systems with meta-control could advance autonomous AI learning beyond current limitations, providing a pathway toward more adaptive, flexible artificial intelligence systems.

title: Chunk-Guided Q-Learning
summary: In offline reinforcement learning (RL), single-step temporal-difference (TD) learning can suffer from bootstrapping error accumulation over long horizons. Action-chunked TD methods mitigate this by backing up over multiple steps, but can introduce suboptimality by restricting the policy class to open-loop action sequences. To resolve this trade-off, we present Chunk-Guided Q-Learning (CGQ), a single-step TD algorithm that guides a fine-grained single-step critic by regularizing it toward a chunk-based critic trained using temporally extended backups. This reduces compounding error while preserving fine-grained value propagation. We theoretically show that CGQ attains tighter critic optimality bounds than either single-step or action-chunked TD learning alone. Empirically, CGQ achieves strong performance on challenging long-horizon OGBench tasks, often outperforming both single-step and action-chunked methods.

AI.tldr: CGQ is a novel offline RL method that combines single-step TD learning with action-chunking guidance to reduce bootstrapping error while maintaining fine-grained value propagation.
AI.motivation: Offline RL suffers from bootstrapping error accumulation over long horizons with single-step TD learning, while action-chunked TD methods can introduce suboptimality by restricting policies to open-loop action sequences.
AI.method: Chunk-Guided Q-Learning (CGQ) regularizes a fine-grained single-step critic toward a chunk-based critic trained using temporally extended backups, reducing compounding error while preserving fine-grained value propagation.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows CGQ attains tighter critic optimality bounds than single-step or action-chunked TD alone. Empirically, CGQ achieves strong performance on challenging long-horizon OGBench tasks, often outperforming both baselines.
AI.conclusion: CGQ successfully resolves the trade-off between bootstrapping error and policy flexibility in offline RL, providing a principled approach that combines the benefits of both single-step and multi-step temporal-difference learning.

title: A Hybrid Modeling Framework for Crop Prediction Tasks via Dynamic Parameter Calibration and Multi-Task Learning
summary: Accurate prediction of crop states (e.g., phenology stages and cold hardiness) is essential for timely farm management decisions such as irrigation, fertilization, and canopy management to optimize crop yield and quality. While traditional biophysical models can be used for season-long predictions, they lack the precision required for site-specific management. Deep learning methods are a compelling alternative, but can produce biologically unrealistic predictions and require large-scale data. We propose a \emph{hybrid modeling} approach that uses a neural network to parameterize a differentiable biophysical model and leverages multi-task learning for efficient data sharing across crop cultivars in data limited settings. By predicting the \emph{parameters} of the biophysical model, our approach improves the prediction accuracy while preserving biological realism. Empirical evaluation using real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrates that our method improves prediction accuracy by 60\% for phenology and 40\% for cold hardiness compared to deployed biophysical models.

AI.tldr: A hybrid modeling approach that uses neural networks to parameterize differentiable biophysical models with multi-task learning improves crop state prediction accuracy while maintaining biological realism in data-limited settings.
AI.motivation: Traditional biophysical models lack precision for site-specific crop management, while deep learning methods can produce biologically unrealistic predictions and require large datasets. There's a need for accurate, biologically realistic crop state predictions for farm management decisions.
AI.method: A hybrid modeling approach using neural networks to parameterize a differentiable biophysical model, combined with multi-task learning for efficient data sharing across different crop cultivars in data-limited settings.
AI.result: The method improves prediction accuracy by 60% for phenology and 40% for cold hardiness compared to deployed biophysical models, as demonstrated on real-world and synthetic datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid approach successfully combines the strengths of neural networks and biophysical models to achieve more accurate and biologically realistic crop state predictions, addressing limitations of both traditional approaches.

title: Aumann-SHAP: The Geometry of Counterfactual Interaction Explanations in Machine Learning
summary: We introduce Aumann-SHAP, an interaction-aware framework that decomposes counterfactual transitions by restricting the model to a local hypercube connecting baseline and counterfactual features. Each hyper-cube is decomposed into a grid in order to construct an induced micro-player cooperative game in which elementary grid-step moves become players. Shapley and LES values on this TU-micro-game yield: (i) within-pot contribution of each feature to the interaction with other features (interaction explainability), and (ii) the contribution of each instance and each feature to the counterfactual analysis (individual and global explainability). In particular, Aumann-LES values produce individual and global explanations along the counterfactual transition. Shapley and LES values converge to the diagonal Aumann-Shapley (integrated-gradients) attribution method. Experiments on the German Credit dataset and MNIST data show that Aumann-LES produces robust results and better explanations than the standard Shapley value during the counterfactual transition.
