AI.result: Harmful prompt injection during TTRL amplifies model's existing behaviors: safety amplification for safe base models, harmfulness amplification for vulnerable models, with reasoning decline ("reasoning tax"). Adversarial "HarmInject" prompts can force models to answer jailbreak and reasoning queries together, causing stronger harmfulness amplification.
AI.conclusion: TTT methods that enhance LLM reasoning through self-consistency can lead to amplification behaviors and reasoning degradation, highlighting need for safer TTT methods that can handle prompt injection vulnerabilities.

title: Evaluating the Robustness of Reinforcement Learning based Adaptive Traffic Signal Control
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has attracted increasing interest for adaptive traffic signal control due to its model-free ability to learn control policies directly from interaction with the traffic environment. However, several challenges remain before RL-based signal control can be considered ready for field deployment. Many existing studies rely on simplified signal timing structures, robustness of trained models under varying traffic demand conditions remains insufficiently evaluated, and runtime efficiency continues to pose challenges when training RL algorithms in traffic microscopic simulation environments. This study formulates an RL-based signal control algorithm capable of representing a full eight-phase ring-barrier configuration consistent with field signal controllers. The algorithm is trained and evaluated under varying traffic demand conditions and benchmarked against state-of-the-practice actuated signal control (ASC). To assess robustness, experiments are conducted across multiple traffic volumes and origin-destination (O-D) demand patterns with varying levels of structural similarity. To improve training efficiency, a distributed asynchronous training architecture is implemented that enables parallel simulation across multiple computing nodes. Results from a case study intersection show that the proposed RL-based signal control significantly outperforms optimized ASC, reducing average delay by 11-32% across movements. A model trained on a single O-D pattern generalizes well to similar unseen demand patterns but degrades under substantially different demand conditions. In contrast, a model trained on diverse O-D patterns demonstrates strong robustness, consistently outperforming ASC even under highly dissimilar unseen demand scenarios.

AI.tldr: RL-based traffic signal control with full eight-phase ring-barrier configuration outperforms actuated signal control, reduces delays by 11-32%, and demonstrates robust generalization when trained on diverse traffic patterns.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based signal control studies use simplified timing structures, lack robustness evaluation under varying traffic conditions, and suffer from poor runtime efficiency during training in simulation environments.
AI.method: Developed RL algorithm representing full eight-phase ring-barrier configuration consistent with field controllers, implemented distributed asynchronous training architecture for parallel simulation, and evaluated robustness across multiple traffic volumes and O-D patterns.
AI.result: RL control significantly outperforms optimized actuated signal control, reducing average delay by 11-32%. Models trained on single patterns generalize to similar unseen patterns but degrade under substantially different conditions, while models trained on diverse patterns show strong robustness across dissimilar unseen scenarios.
AI.conclusion: RL-based signal control with realistic timing structures and distributed training can achieve superior performance and robust generalization, making it promising for field deployment when trained on diverse traffic patterns.

title: Physics-informed fine-tuning of foundation models for partial differential equations
summary: Foundation models for partial differential equations (PDEs) have emerged as powerful surrogates pre-trained on diverse physical systems, but adapting them to new downstream tasks remains challenging due to limited task-specific data and distribution shifts. While fine-tuning has proven transformative in natural language processing, best practices for adapting PDE foundation models remain underexplored. Although physics-informed training has successfully trained accurate solvers across a wide range of PDE problems, its potential for fine-tuning data-based foundation models has not been systematically studied. In this work, we introduce a physics-informed fine-tuning framework that adapts pre-trained PDE foundation models by incorporating physical constraints (PDE residuals and boundary conditions) directly into the fine-tuning objective. This enables effective adaptation in data-scarce regimes while promoting physical consistency. We evaluate our method on a downstream task composed of an unseen PDE class and compare it with data-driven finetuning counterparts. Our results demonstrate that physics-informed fine-tuning achieves competitive accuracy without requiring PDE solutions for training. Furthermore, a hybrid fine-tuning strategy yields superior generalization to out-of-distribution scenarios when only minimal training data is available. These findings establish physics-informed fine-tuning as a scalable and data-efficient paradigm, providing a physically interpretable pathway for adapting foundation models in scientific machine learning.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed fine-tuning adapts PDE foundation models using physical constraints (PDE residuals and boundary conditions) instead of just data, enabling effective adaptation with limited task-specific data while maintaining physical consistency.
AI.motivation: Current PDE foundation models struggle with adapting to new downstream tasks due to limited task-specific data and distribution shifts. While fine-tuning works well in NLP, best practices for adapting PDE foundation models remain underexplored. Physics-informed training has shown success for PDE solvers, but its potential for fine-tuning data-based foundation models hasn't been systematically studied.
AI.method: Introduces a physics-informed fine-tuning framework that adapts pre-trained PDE foundation models by incorporating physical constraints (PDE residuals and boundary conditions) directly into the fine-tuning objective. This approach enables effective adaptation in data-scarce regimes while promoting physical consistency. Also explores a hybrid fine-tuning strategy combining physical constraints with minimal data.
AI.result: Physics-informed fine-tuning achieves competitive accuracy without requiring PDE solutions for training. The hybrid fine-tuning strategy yields superior generalization to out-of-distribution scenarios when only minimal training data is available. The method effectively adapts to unseen PDE classes in downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed fine-tuning establishes a scalable and data-efficient paradigm for adapting PDE foundation models, providing a physically interpretable pathway that enables effective adaptation with limited data while maintaining physical consistency.

title: Enhancing classification accuracy through chaos
summary: We propose a novel approach which exploits chaos to enhance classification accuracy. Specifically, the available data that need to be classified are treated as vectors that are first lifted into a higher-dimensional space and then used as initial conditions for the evolution of a chaotic dynamical system for a prescribed temporal interval. The evolved state of the dynamical system is then fed to a trainable softmax classifier which outputs the probabilities of the various classes. As proof-of-concept, we use samples of randomly perturbed orthogonal vectors of moderate dimension (2 to 20), with a corresponding number of classes equal to the vector dimension, and show how our approach can both significantly accelerate the training process and improve the classification accuracy compared to a standard softmax classifier which operates on the original vectors, as well as a softmax classifier which only lifts the vectors to a higher-dimensional space without evolving them. We also provide an explanation for the improved performance of the chaos-enhanced classifier.

AI.tldr: Chaos-enhanced classification: lifting data vectors to higher-dimensional space, evolving them through chaotic dynamics, then feeding to softmax classifier improves accuracy and training speed compared to standard approaches.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to improve classification accuracy and accelerate training processes by leveraging chaotic dynamics, showing that standard softmax classifiers can be enhanced through chaotic evolution of data vectors.
AI.method: Data vectors are lifted into a higher-dimensional space and used as initial conditions for chaotic dynamical system evolution over a prescribed temporal interval. The evolved state is then fed to a trainable softmax classifier.
AI.result: For randomly perturbed orthogonal vectors (2-20 dimensions), the chaos-enhanced approach significantly accelerates training and improves classification accuracy compared to both standard softmax classifiers and classifiers that only lift vectors without chaotic evolution.
AI.conclusion: Chaotic dynamics can effectively enhance classification performance by providing richer representations through evolutionary transformation in higher-dimensional spaces, offering a novel approach to improve machine learning classifiers.

title: TabKD: Tabular Knowledge Distillation through Interaction Diversity of Learned Feature Bins
summary: Data-free knowledge distillation enables model compression without original training data, critical for privacy-sensitive tabular domains. However, existing methods does not perform well on tabular data because they do not explicitly address feature interactions, the fundamental way tabular models encode predictive knowledge. We identify interaction diversity, systematic coverage of feature combinations, as an essential requirement for effective tabular distillation. To operationalize this insight, we propose TabKD, which learns adaptive feature bins aligned with teacher decision boundaries, then generates synthetic queries that maximize pairwise interaction coverage. Across 4 benchmark datasets and 4 teacher architectures, TabKD achieves highest student-teacher agreement in 14 out of 16 configurations, outperforming 5 state-of-the-art baselines. We further show that interaction coverage strongly correlates with distillation quality, validating our core hypothesis. Our work establishes interaction-focused exploration as a principled framework for tabular model extraction.

AI.tldr: TabKD introduces a novel data-free knowledge distillation method for tabular data that focuses on feature interaction coverage, outperforming existing baselines by generating synthetic queries that maximize pairwise interaction diversity.
AI.motivation: Existing data-free knowledge distillation methods perform poorly on tabular data because they don't address feature interactions, which are fundamental to how tabular models encode predictive knowledge. The authors identified interaction diversity (systematic coverage of feature combinations) as essential for effective tabular distillation.
AI.method: TabKD learns adaptive feature bins aligned with teacher decision boundaries, then generates synthetic queries that maximize pairwise interaction coverage. This creates diverse feature combinations to facilitate effective knowledge transfer from teacher to student models.
AI.result: Across 4 benchmark datasets and 4 teacher architectures, TabKD achieved highest student-teacher agreement in 14 out of 16 configurations, outperforming 5 state-of-the-art baselines. Interaction coverage strongly correlated with distillation quality, validating the core hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes interaction-focused exploration as a principled framework for tabular model extraction, demonstrating that explicit attention to feature interaction diversity is crucial for effective data-free knowledge distillation in tabular domains.

title: xplainfi: Feature Importance and Statistical Inference for Machine Learning in R
summary: We introduce xplainfi, an R package built on top of the mlr3 ecosystem for global, loss-based feature importance methods for machine learning models. Various feature importance methods exist in R, but significant gaps remain, particularly regarding conditional importance methods and associated statistical inference procedures. The package implements permutation feature importance, conditional feature importance, relative feature importance, leave-one-covariate-out, and generalizations thereof, and both marginal and conditional Shapley additive global importance methods. It provides a modular conditional sampling architecture based on Gaussian distributions, adversarial random forests, conditional inference trees, and knockoff-based samplers, which enable conditional importance analysis for continuous and mixed data. Statistical inference is available through multiple approaches, including variance-corrected confidence intervals and the conditional predictive impact framework. We demonstrate that xplainfi produces importance scores consistent with existing implementations across multiple simulation settings and learner types, while offering competitive runtime performance. The package is available on CRAN and provides researchers and practitioners with a comprehensive toolkit for feature importance analysis and model interpretation in R.

AI.tldr: xplainfi is an R package for global, loss-based feature importance methods built on mlr3, offering various importance methods with conditional sampling and statistical inference capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing R feature importance methods have significant gaps, particularly regarding conditional importance methods and associated statistical inference procedures.
AI.method: The package implements permutation feature importance, conditional feature importance, relative feature importance, leave-one-covariate-out, and both marginal and conditional Shapley additive global importance methods. It provides a modular conditional sampling architecture using Gaussian distributions, adversarial random forests, conditional inference trees, and knockoff-based samplers.
AI.result: xplainfi produces importance scores consistent with existing implementations across multiple simulation settings and learner types, while offering competitive runtime performance.
AI.conclusion: xplainfi provides researchers and practitioners with a comprehensive toolkit for feature importance analysis and model interpretation in R, available on CRAN.

title: Grokking as a Variance-Limited Phase Transition: Spectral Gating and the Epsilon-Stability Threshold
summary:
Standard optimization theories struggle to explain grokking, where generalization occurs long after training convergence. While geometric studies attribute this to slow drift, they often overlook the interaction between the optimizer's noise structure and landscape curvature. This work analyzes AdamW dynamics on modular arithmetic tasks, revealing a ``Spectral Gating'' mechanism that regulates the transition from memorization to generalization.
  We find that AdamW operates as a variance-gated stochastic system. Grokking is constrained by a stability condition: the generalizing solution resides in a sharp basin ($λ_{max}^H$) initially inaccessible under low-variance regimes. The ``delayed'' phase represents the accumulation of gradient variance required to lift the effective stability ceiling, permitting entry into this sharp manifold.
  Our ablation studies identify three complexity regimes: (1) \textbf{Capacity Collapse} ($P < 23$), where rank-deficiency prevents structural learning; (2) \textbf{The Variance-Limited Regime} ($P \approx 41$), where generalization waits for the spectral gate to open; and (3) \textbf{Stability Override} ($P > 67$), where memorization becomes dimensionally unstable. Furthermore, we challenge the "Flat Minima" hypothesis for algorithmic tasks, showing that isotropic noise injection fails to induce grokking. Generalization requires the \textit{anisotropic rectification} unique to adaptive optimizers, which directs noise into the tangent space of the solution manifold.

AI.tldr: Grokking in AdamW is explained by a 'Spectral Gating' mechanism: generalization occurs after accumulating enough gradient variance to enter a sharp solution basin. Anisotropic noise from adaptive optimizers is crucial, while isotropic noise fails. Three complexity regimes determine grokking behavior.
AI.motivation: Standard optimization theories fail to explain the phenomenon of grokking—where generalization occurs long after training convergence. Prior geometric studies attribute grokking to slow drift but often overlook the critical interaction between the optimizer's noise structure and the curvature of the loss landscape. This work aims to uncover the underlying mechanism driving grokking in adaptive optimizers like AdamW, particularly on algorithmic tasks such as modular arithmetic.
AI.method: The work analyzes AdamW dynamics on modular arithmetic tasks, employing theoretical and empirical approaches. It models AdamW as a variance-gated stochastic system and examines the interaction between optimizer noise and landscape curvature. Ablation studies are conducted to explore different complexity regimes (varying parameter P) and to test the effects of isotropic versus anisotropic noise injection.
AI.result: The study reveals a 'Spectral Gating' mechanism in AdamW dynamics that regulates the transition from memorization to generalization. Grokking is constrained by a stability condition: the generalizing solution lies in a sharp basin (λ_max^H) initially inaccessible under low-variance regimes. The 'delayed' phase corresponds to the accumulation of gradient variance needed to lift the effective stability ceiling, allowing entry into this sharp manifold. Ablation studies identify three complexity regimes: (1) Capacity Collapse (P < 23), where rank-deficiency prevents structural learning; (2) The Variance-Limited Regime (P ≈ 41), where generalization waits for the spectral gate to open; and (3) Stability Override (P > 67), where memorization becomes dimensionally unstable. Additionally, the work challenges the 'Flat Minima' hypothesis for algorithmic tasks, showing isotropic noise injection fails to induce grokking; generalization requires the anisotropic rectification unique to adaptive optimizers, which directs noise into the tangent space of the solution manifold.
AI.conclusion: Grokking in adaptive optimizers like AdamW is governed by a Spectral Gating mechanism, where generalization is delayed until sufficient gradient variance accumulates to overcome stability barriers. The anisotropic noise structure of adaptive optimizers—not isotropic noise—is essential for directing exploration into the tangent space of the solution manifold, challenging the flat minima hypothesis for algorithmic tasks. The findings delineate distinct complexity regimes that dictate whether and when grokking occurs.

title: A Kolmogorov-Arnold Surrogate Model for Chemical Equilibria: Application to Solid Solutions
summary: The computational cost of geochemical solvers is a challenging matter. For reactive transport simulations, where chemical calculations are performed up to billions of times, it is crucial to reduce the total computational time. Existing publications have explored various machine-learning approaches to determine the most effective data-driven surrogate model. In particular, multilayer perceptrons are widely employed due to their ability to recognize nonlinear relationships. In this work, we focus on the recent Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, where learnable spline-based functions replace classical fixed activation functions. This architecture has achieved higher accuracy with fewer trainable parameters and has become increasingly popular for solving partial differential equations. First, we train a surrogate model based on an existing cement system benchmark. Then, we move to an application case for the geological disposal of nuclear waste, i.e., the determination of radionuclide-bearing solids solubilities. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to investigate co-precipitation with radionuclide incorporation using data-driven surrogate models, considering increasing levels of thermodynamic complexity from simple mechanical mixtures to non-ideal solid solutions of binary (Ba,Ra)SO$_4$ and ternary (Sr,Ba,Ra)SO$_4$ systems. On the cement benchmark, we demonstrate that the Kolmogorov-Arnold architecture outperforms multilayer perceptrons in both absolute and relative error metrics, reducing them by 62% and 59%, respectively. On the binary and ternary radium solid solution models, Kolmogorov-Arnold networks maintain median prediction errors near $1\times10^{-3}$. This is the first step toward employing surrogate models to speed up reactive transport simulations and optimize the safety assessment of deep geological waste repositories.

AI.tldr: Kolmogorov-Arnold networks outperform traditional multilayer perceptrons as data-driven surrogate models for geochemical solvers, reducing errors by 60% and maintaining high accuracy for complex radionuclide co-precipitation systems.
AI.motivation: Geochemical solvers are computationally expensive, especially for reactive transport simulations requiring billions of chemical calculations, creating a need for efficient surrogate models to reduce computational time.
AI.method: The study uses Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) with learnable spline-based activation functions instead of traditional fixed activation functions. The approach is tested on an existing cement system benchmark and applied to geological nuclear waste disposal scenarios, specifically modeling radionuclide-bearing solids solubilities in binary (Ba,Ra)SO4 and ternary (Sr,Ba,Ra)SO4 solid solutions.
AI.result: On the cement benchmark, KANs outperformed multilayer perceptrons, reducing absolute and relative errors by 62% and 59% respectively. For binary and ternary radium solid solution models, KANs maintained median prediction errors near 1×10⁻³.
AI.conclusion: Kolmogorov-Arnold networks represent a promising approach for developing accurate surrogate models to accelerate reactive transport simulations, with potential applications in optimizing safety assessments for deep geological waste repositories.

title: Seeking SOTA: Time-Series Forecasting Must Adopt Taxonomy-Specific Evaluation to Dispel Illusory Gains
comment: Position paper; 8 figures, 8 tables; includes appendix
summary: We argue that the current practice of evaluating AI/ML time-series forecasting models, predominantly on benchmarks characterized by strong, persistent periodicities and seasonalities, obscures real progress by overlooking the performance of efficient classical methods. We demonstrate that these "standard" datasets often exhibit dominant autocorrelation patterns and seasonal cycles that can be effectively captured by simpler linear or statistical models, rendering complex deep learning architectures frequently no more performant than their classical counterparts for these specific data characteristics, and raising questions as to whether any marginal improvements justify the significant increase in computational overhead and model complexity. We call on the community to (I) retire or substantially augment current benchmarks with datasets exhibiting a wider spectrum of non-stationarities, such as structural breaks, time-varying volatility, and concept drift, and less predictable dynamics drawn from diverse real-world domains, and (II) require every deep learning submission to include robust classical and simple baselines, appropriately chosen for the specific characteristics of the downstream tasks' time series. By doing so, we will help ensure that reported gains reflect genuine scientific methodological advances rather than artifacts of benchmark selection favoring models adept at learning repetitive patterns.

AI.tldr: Current time-series forecasting benchmarks overemphasize periodic data, making deep learning models seem no better than simpler classical methods. The authors call for more diverse benchmarks and mandatory classical baselines to ensure real progress is measured.
AI.motivation: The paper argues that current evaluation practices for AI/ML time-series forecasting models are flawed because they predominantly use benchmarks with strong periodicities and seasonalities. This obscures real progress by overlooking the performance of efficient classical methods, as these datasets can be effectively captured by simpler models, making complex deep learning architectures appear no better while incurring significant computational overhead.
AI.method: The paper uses analytical argumentation and empirical demonstration to show that current benchmarks favor models that excel at learning repetitive patterns. It calls for community action to retire or augment benchmarks with datasets exhibiting a wider spectrum of non-stationarities (e.g., structural breaks, time-varying volatility, concept drift) and less predictable dynamics from diverse real-world domains.
AI.result: The authors demonstrate that standard datasets often have dominant autocorrelation patterns and seasonal cycles that simpler linear or statistical models can capture effectively. Consequently, complex deep learning models frequently show no significant performance advantage over classical counterparts for these data characteristics, raising questions about whether marginal improvements justify increased computational costs.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that the community should (I) retire or substantially augment current benchmarks with more diverse and challenging datasets, and (II) require deep learning submissions to include robust classical and simple baselines tailored to the specific characteristics of the time series. This will ensure reported gains reflect genuine methodological advances rather than benchmark selection artifacts.

title: CASHomon Sets: Efficient Rashomon Sets Across Multiple Model Classes and their Hyperparameters
comment: Equal contributions by Fiona Katharina Ewald and Martin Binder
summary: Rashomon sets are model sets within one model class that perform nearly as well as a reference model from the same model class. They reveal the existence of alternative well-performing models, which may support different interpretations. This enables selecting models that match domain knowledge, hidden constraints, or user preferences. However, efficient construction methods currently exist for only a few model classes. Applied machine learning usually searches many model classes, and the best class is unknown beforehand. We therefore study Rashomon sets in the combined algorithm selection and hyperparameter optimization (CASH) setting and call them CASHomon sets. We propose TruVaRImp, a model-based active learning algorithm for level set estimation with an implicit threshold, and provide convergence guarantees. On synthetic and real-world datasets, TruVaRImp reliably identifies CASHomon sets members and matches or outperforms naive sampling, Bayesian optimization, classical and implicit level set estimation methods, and other baselines. Our analyses of predictive multiplicity and feature-importance variability across model classes question the common practice of interpreting data through a single model class.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CASHomon sets (Rashomon sets for combined algorithm selection and hyperparameter optimization) and proposes TruVaRImp, an active learning algorithm to efficiently identify alternative well-performing models across different model classes, challenging the practice of interpreting data through a single model class.
AI.motivation: Current Rashomon set construction methods are limited to few model classes, but applied ML typically searches many model classes where the best class is unknown beforehand. This creates a need to study Rashomon sets in the CASH (combined algorithm selection and hyperparameter optimization) setting to find alternative well-performing models across different model classes.
AI.method: The paper proposes TruVaRImp, a model-based active learning algorithm for level set estimation with an implicit threshold, which efficiently identifies CASHomon set members. The method provides convergence guarantees and is evaluated against various baselines including naive sampling, Bayesian optimization, and classical/implicit level set estimation methods.
AI.result: On synthetic and real-world datasets, TruVaRImp reliably identifies CASHomon set members and matches or outperforms naive sampling, Bayesian optimization, classical and implicit level set estimation methods, and other baselines. The analyses reveal significant predictive multiplicity and feature-importance variability across model classes.
AI.conclusion: The existence of CASHomon sets and the demonstrated variability in predictive multiplicity and feature importance across model classes question the common practice of interpreting data through a single model class. The proposed TruVaRImp algorithm provides an effective way to explore alternative well-performing models across different model classes.

title: Building Trust in PINNs: Error Estimation through Finite Difference Methods
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) constitute a flexible deep learning approach for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), which model phenomena ranging from heat conduction to quantum mechanical systems. Despite their flexibility, PINNs offer limited insight into how their predictions deviate from the true solution, hindering trust in their prediction quality. We propose a lightweight post-hoc method that addresses this gap by producing pointwise error estimates for PINN predictions, which offer a natural form of explanation for such models, identifying not just whether a prediction is wrong, but where and by how much. For linear partial differential equations, the error between a PINN approximation and the true solution satisfies the same differential operator as the original problem, but driven by the PINN's PDE residual as its source term. We solve this error equation numerically using finite difference methods requiring no knowledge of the true solution. Evaluated on several benchmark PDEs, our method yields accurate error maps at low computational cost, enabling targeted and interpretable validation of PINNs.

AI.tldr: A lightweight post-hoc method for producing pointwise error estimates for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) predictions, enabling interpretable validation by solving the error equation using finite difference methods.
AI.motivation: PINNs offer limited insight into how their predictions deviate from true solutions, hindering trust in prediction quality. There's a need for methods that provide error estimates to identify not just whether predictions are wrong, but where and by how much.
AI.method: For linear PDEs, the error between PINN approximation and true solution satisfies the same differential operator as the original problem, driven by the PINN's PDE residual as its source term. This error equation is solved numerically using finite difference methods without requiring knowledge of the true solution.
AI.result: The method yields accurate error maps at low computational cost when evaluated on several benchmark PDEs. It enables targeted and interpretable validation of PINNs by identifying specific regions and magnitudes of prediction errors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight post-hoc method successfully addresses the trust gap in PINNs by providing pointwise error estimates, offering a natural form of explanation that enhances interpretability and validation capabilities for PINN predictions.

title: Data Augmentation via Causal-Residual Bootstrapping
summary: Data augmentation integrates domain knowledge into a dataset by making domain-informed modifications to existing data points. For example, image data can be augmented by duplicating images in different tints or orientations, thereby incorporating the knowledge that images may vary in these dimensions. Recent work by Teshima and Sugiyama has explored the integration of causal knowledge (e.g, A causes B causes C) up to conditional independence equivalence. We suggest a related approach for settings with additive noise that can incorporate information beyond a Markov equivalence class. The approach, built on the principle of independent mechanisms, permutes the residuals of models built on marginal probability distributions. Predictive models built on our augmented data demonstrate improved accuracy, for which we provide theoretical backing in linear Gaussian settings.

AI.tldr: A data augmentation method using independent mechanisms and residual permutation to incorporate causal knowledge beyond Markov equivalence, improving predictive model accuracy with theoretical backing in linear Gaussian settings.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to incorporate causal knowledge beyond Markov equivalence classes into data augmentation, particularly for additive noise settings, to improve predictive model accuracy.
AI.method: The approach is based on the principle of independent mechanisms, permuting the residuals of models built on marginal probability distributions.
AI.result: Predictive models built on the augmented data show improved accuracy, with theoretical support provided for linear Gaussian settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed data augmentation method effectively integrates causal knowledge using independent mechanisms and residual permutation, enhancing predictive accuracy with theoretical guarantees in linear Gaussian cases.

title: The PokeAgent Challenge: Competitive and Long-Context Learning at Scale
comment: 41 pages, 26 figures, 5 tables. NeurIPS 2025 Competition Track
summary: We present the PokeAgent Challenge, a large-scale benchmark for decision-making research built on Pokemon's multi-agent battle system and expansive role-playing game (RPG) environment. Partial observability, game-theoretic reasoning, and long-horizon planning remain open problems for frontier AI, yet few benchmarks stress all three simultaneously under realistic conditions. PokeAgent targets these limitations at scale through two complementary tracks: our Battling Track, which calls for strategic reasoning and generalization under partial observability in competitive Pokemon battles, and our Speedrunning Track, which requires long-horizon planning and sequential decision-making in the Pokemon RPG. Our Battling Track supplies a dataset of 20M+ battle trajectories alongside a suite of heuristic, RL, and LLM-based baselines capable of high-level competitive play. Our Speedrunning Track provides the first standardized evaluation framework for RPG speedrunning, including an open-source multi-agent orchestration system for modular, reproducible comparisons of harness-based LLM approaches. Our NeurIPS 2025 competition validates both the quality of our resources and the research community's interest in Pokemon, with over 100 teams competing across both tracks and winning solutions detailed in our paper. Participant submissions and our baselines reveal considerable gaps between generalist (LLM), specialist (RL), and elite human performance. Analysis against the BenchPress evaluation matrix shows that Pokemon battling is nearly orthogonal to standard LLM benchmarks, measuring capabilities not captured by existing suites and positioning Pokemon as an unsolved benchmark that can drive RL and LLM research forward. We transition to a living benchmark with a live leaderboard for Battling and self-contained evaluation for Speedrunning at https://pokeagentchallenge.com.

AI.tldr: PokeAgent Challenge is a large-scale benchmark for decision-making research using Pokemon's battle system and RPG environment, featuring battling and speedrunning tracks to test strategic reasoning, partial observability, and long-horizon planning.
AI.motivation: Partial observability, game-theoretic reasoning, and long-horizon planning remain open problems for AI, but few benchmarks test all three simultaneously under realistic conditions. Existing benchmarks don't adequately stress these capabilities together.
AI.method: Created two complementary tracks: 1) Battling Track with 20M+ battle trajectories and baselines (heuristic, RL, LLM-based) for strategic reasoning under partial observability, and 2) Speedrunning Track with standardized evaluation framework and multi-agent orchestration system for RPG speedrunning.
AI.result: NeurIPS 2025 competition with 100+ teams validated community interest. Analysis shows Pokemon battling is nearly orthogonal to standard LLM benchmarks, measuring capabilities not captured by existing suites. Considerable gaps exist between generalist (LLM), specialist (RL), and elite human performance.
AI.conclusion: PokeAgent Challenge positions Pokemon as an unsolved benchmark that can drive RL and LLM research forward. It has transitioned to a living benchmark with live leaderboard for Battling and self-contained evaluation for Speedrunning, providing ongoing research value.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Systems for the Simulation of EUV Electromagnetic Wave Diffraction from a Lithography Mask
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2507.04153
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and neural operators (NOs) for solving the problem of diffraction of Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) electromagnetic waves from contemporary lithography masks are presented. A novel hybrid Waveguide Neural Operator (WGNO) is introduced, based on a waveguide method with its most computationally expensive components replaced by a neural network. To evaluate performance, the accuracy and inference time of PINNs and NOs are compared against modern numerical solvers for a series of problems with known exact solutions. The emphasis is placed on investigation of solution accuracy by considered artificial neural systems for 13.5 nm and 11.2 nm wavelengths. Numerical experiments on realistic 2D and 3D masks demonstrate that PINNs and neural operators achieve competitive accuracy and significantly reduced prediction times, with the proposed WGNO architecture reaching state-of-the-art performance. The presented neural operator has pronounced generalizing properties, meaning that for unseen problem parameters it delivers a solution accuracy close to that for parameters seen in the training dataset. These results provide a highly efficient solution for accelerating the design and optimization workflows of next-generation lithography masks.

AI.tldr: PINNs and neural operators are applied to EUV lithography mask diffraction problems, with a hybrid Waveguide Neural Operator achieving competitive accuracy and significantly faster prediction times compared to numerical solvers.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to accelerate the design and optimization workflows for next-generation lithography masks by leveraging physics-informed neural networks and neural operators to solve EUV electromagnetic wave diffraction problems more efficiently than traditional numerical methods.
AI.method: The paper presents physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) and neural operators (NOs) for solving EUV diffraction problems. It introduces a novel hybrid Waveguide Neural Operator (WGNO) based on a waveguide method with computationally expensive components replaced by neural networks. The accuracy and inference time of PINNs and NOs are compared against modern numerical solvers using problems with known exact solutions.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on realistic 2D and 3D masks show that PINNs and neural operators achieve competitive accuracy with significantly reduced prediction times. The WGNO architecture reaches state-of-the-art performance and demonstrates good generalization properties for unseen problem parameters.
AI.conclusion: The presented neural operator approach provides a highly efficient solution for accelerating design and optimization workflows of next-generation lithography masks, offering competitive accuracy and faster computation compared to traditional numerical methods.

title: Deep learning and the rate of approximation by flows
summary: We investigate the dependence of the approximation capacity of deep residual networks on its depth in a continuous dynamical systems setting. This can be formulated as the general problem of quantifying the minimal time-horizon required to approximate a diffeomorphism by flows driven by a given family $\mathcal F$ of vector fields. We show that this minimal time can be identified as a geodesic distance on a sub-Finsler manifold of diffeomorphisms, where the local geometry is characterised by a variational principle involving $\mathcal F$. This connects the learning efficiency of target relationships to their compatibility with the learning architectural choice. Further, the results suggest that the key approximation mechanism in deep learning, namely the approximation of functions by composition or dynamics, differs in a fundamental way from linear approximation theory, where linear spaces and norm-based rate estimates are replaced by manifolds and geodesic distances.

AI.tldr: Deep residual networks' approximation capacity depends on depth via minimal time to approximate diffeomorphisms by flows of vector fields, connecting learning efficiency to architectural compatibility.
AI.motivation: To understand how the approximation capacity of deep residual networks depends on their depth, using continuous dynamical systems formulation to quantify the minimal time needed to approximate diffeomorphisms by flows of given vector fields.
AI.method: Formulate the problem as quantifying minimal time-horizon to approximate diffeomorphisms by flows driven by a given family of vector fields. Show this minimal time equals geodesic distance on a sub-Finsler manifold of diffeomorphisms, with local geometry characterized by variational principles involving the vector field family.
AI.result: The minimal time required for approximation corresponds to a geodesic distance on a sub-Finsler manifold, connecting learning efficiency to architectural compatibility. This reveals that deep learning's approximation mechanism (function composition/dynamics) fundamentally differs from linear approximation theory.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning's approximation mechanism operates on manifolds with geodesic distances rather than linear spaces with norm-based rates, showing fundamental differences from linear approximation theory and connecting learning efficiency to architectural choices.

title: Local Urysohn Width: A Topological Complexity Measure for Classification
summary: We introduce \emph{local Urysohn width}, a complexity measure for classification problems on metric spaces. Unlike VC dimension, fat-shattering dimension, and Rademacher complexity, which characterize the richness of hypothesis \emph{classes}, Urysohn width characterizes the topological-geometric complexity of the classification \emph{problem itself}: the minimum number of connected, diameter-bounded local experts needed to correctly classify all points within a margin-safe region. We prove four main results. First, a \textbf{strict hierarchy theorem}: for every integer $w \geq 1$, there exists a classification problem on a \emph{connected} compact metric space (a bouquet of circles with first Betti number $β_1 = w$) whose Urysohn width is exactly~$w$, establishing that topological complexity of the input space forces classifier complexity. Second, a \textbf{topology $\times$ geometry scaling law}: width scales as $Ω(w \cdot L/D_0)$, where $w$ counts independent loops and $L/D_0$ is the ratio of loop circumference to locality scale. Third, a \textbf{two-way separation from VC dimension}: there exist problem families where width grows unboundedly while VC dimension is bounded by a constant, and conversely, families where VC dimension grows unboundedly while width remains~1. Fourth, a \textbf{sample complexity lower bound}: any learner that must correctly classify all points in the safe region of a width-$w$ problem needs $Ω(w \log w)$ samples, independent of VC dimension.

AI.tldr: Urysohn width is a new complexity measure for classification problems that captures topological-geometric complexity of the problem itself, showing strict hierarchy, scaling laws, separation from VC dimension, and sample complexity lower bounds.
AI.motivation: Existing complexity measures like VC dimension characterize hypothesis classes, but there's a need for a measure that captures the inherent topological-geometric complexity of the classification problem itself, independent of the hypothesis class.
AI.method: Introduces local Urysohn width as the minimum number of connected, diameter-bounded local experts needed to correctly classify all points within a margin-safe region. Proves four main results through theoretical analysis and construction of specific examples.
AI.result: 1) Strict hierarchy theorem showing Urysohn width exactly equals topological complexity; 2) Topology × geometry scaling law; 3) Two-way separation from VC dimension; 4) Sample complexity lower bound of Ω(w log w) samples for width-w problems.
AI.conclusion: Urysohn width provides a fundamentally different perspective on classification complexity that captures topological-geometric structure, revealing limitations of traditional measures and establishing new theoretical foundations for understanding classification difficulty.

title: Federated Learning of Binary Neural Networks: Enabling Low-Cost Inference
summary: Federated Learning (FL) preserves privacy by distributing training across devices. However, using DNNs is computationally intensive at the low-powered edge during inference. Edge deployment demands models that simultaneously optimize memory footprint and computational efficiency, a dilemma where conventional DNNs fail by exceeding resource limits. Traditional post-training binarization reduces model size but suffers from severe accuracy loss due to quantization errors. To address these challenges, we propose FedBNN, a rotation-aware binary neural network framework that learns binary representations directly during local training. By encoding each weight as a single bit $\{+1, -1\}$ instead of a $32$-bit float, FedBNN shrinks the model footprint, significantly reducing runtime (during inference) FLOPs and memory requirements in comparison to federated methods using real models. Evaluations across multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that FedBNN significantly reduces resource consumption while performing similarly to existing federated methods using real-valued models.

AI.tldr: FedBNN is a federated learning framework using binary neural networks to reduce model size and computation requirements at the edge while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Federated Learning preserves privacy but DNNs are computationally intensive at low-powered edge devices during inference. Edge deployment needs models that optimize memory footprint and computational efficiency simultaneously, which conventional DNNs fail to do by exceeding resource limits. Traditional post-training binarization reduces model size but suffers severe accuracy loss due to quantization errors.
AI.method: Proposed FedBNN, a rotation-aware binary neural network framework that learns binary representations directly during local training. Each weight is encoded as a single bit {+1, -1} instead of 32-bit float, shrinking model footprint and reducing runtime FLOPs and memory requirements.
AI.result: Evaluations across multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate FedBNN significantly reduces resource consumption while performing similarly to existing federated methods using real-valued models.
AI.conclusion: FedBNN successfully addresses the challenge of deploying computationally intensive models at resource-limited edge devices in federated learning by using binary neural networks that maintain accuracy while drastically reducing resource requirements.

title: Not All Invariants Are Equal: Curating Training Data to Accelerate Program Verification with SLMs
summary: The synthesis of inductive loop invariants is a critical bottleneck in automated program verification. While Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise in mitigating this issue, they often fail on hard instances, generating invariants that are invalid or computationally ineffective. While fine-tuning is a natural route to mitigate this limitation, obtaining high-quality training data for invariant generation remains an open challenge. We present a rigorous data curation pipeline designed to extract high-quality training signals from raw verifier-generated invariants. First, we formalize the properties required for a high-quality training invariant. Second, we propose Wonda, a pipeline that refines noisy data via AST-based normalization, followed by LLM-driven semantic rewriting and augmentation with provable quality guarantees. We demonstrate that fine-tuning Small Language Models (SLMs) on this curated dataset result in consistent and significant performance gain. In particular, a fine-tuned 4B parameter model matches the utility of a GPT-OSS-120B baseline and approaches the state-of-the-art GPT-5.2, without incurring reasoning-time overhead. On challenging instances from the recent InvBench evaluation suite, our approach doubles the invariant correctness and speedup rates of base models; and improves their Virtual Best Performance (VBP) rates on the verification task by up to 14.2%.

AI.tldr: A data curation pipeline (Wonda) extracts high-quality training invariants from verifier-generated data, enabling SLMs to match or approach SOTA LLM performance on invariant synthesis without reasoning-time overhead.
AI.motivation: LLMs often fail on hard instances of invariant synthesis, generating invalid or ineffective invariants. Fine-tuning could help but requires high-quality training data, which is currently lacking.
AI.method: Wonda pipeline: 1) Formalizes properties of high-quality training invariants, 2) Refines noisy data via AST-based normalization, 3) Uses LLM-driven semantic rewriting with provable quality guarantees, 4) Fine-tunes SLMs on curated dataset.
AI.result: Fine-tuned 4B parameter model matches GPT-OSS-120B utility and approaches GPT-5.2 SOTA. On InvBench, doubles correctness/speedup rates of base models, improves VBP rates by up to 14.2%.
AI.conclusion: High-quality curated training data enables SLMs to achieve competitive invariant synthesis performance without reasoning overhead, addressing the data quality bottleneck in automated program verification.

title: Vib2ECG: A Paired Chest-Lead SCG-ECG Dataset and Benchmark for ECG Reconstruction
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Twelve-lead electrocardiography (ECG) is essential for cardiovascular diagnosis, but its long-term acquisition in daily life is constrained by complex and costly hardware. Recent efforts have explored reconstructing ECG from low-cost cardiac vibrational signals such as seismocardiography (SCG), however, due to the lack of a dataset, current methods are limited to limb leads, while clinical diagnosis requires multi-lead ECG, including chest leads. In this work, we propose Vib2ECG, the first paired, multi-channel electro-mechanical cardiac signal dataset, which includes complete twelve-lead ECGs and vibrational signals acquired by inertial measurement units (IMUs) at six chest-lead positions from 17 subjects. Based on this dataset, we also provide a benchmark. Experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of reconstructing electrical cardiac signals at variable locations from vibrational signals using a lightweight 364 K-parameter U-Net. Furthermore, we observe a hallucination phenomenon in the model, where ECG waveforms are generated in regions where no corresponding electrical activity is present. We analyze the causes of this phenomenon and propose potential directions for mitigation. This study demonstrates the feasibility of mobile-device-friendly ECG monitoring through chest-lead ECG prediction from low-cost vibrational signals acquired using IMU sensors. It expands the application of cardiac vibrational signals and provides new insights into the spatial relationship between cardiac electrical and mechanical activities with spatial location variation.

AI.tldr: Vib2ECG introduces first paired dataset of multi-channel cardiac signals (12-lead ECG + vibrational signals) for reconstructing chest-lead ECG from low-cost IMU sensors, but discovers hallucination issues in waveform generation.
AI.motivation: Clinical diagnosis requires multi-lead ECG (including chest leads), but current methods using vibrational signals like SCG are limited to limb leads due to lack of paired datasets. Long-term ECG monitoring in daily life is constrained by complex hardware.
AI.method: Created Vib2ECG dataset with paired 12-lead ECGs and vibrational signals from IMUs at six chest-lead positions from 17 subjects. Used lightweight 364K-parameter U-Net model for ECG reconstruction benchmark.
AI.result: Demonstrated feasibility of reconstructing electrical cardiac signals at variable locations from vibrational signals. Observed hallucination phenomenon where ECG waveforms are generated in regions with no corresponding electrical activity.
AI.conclusion: Study demonstrates feasibility of mobile-device-friendly ECG monitoring through chest-lead ECG prediction from low-cost IMU sensors. Expands application of cardiac vibrational signals and provides insights into spatial relationship between electrical and mechanical cardiac activities.

title: Bridging Local and Global Knowledge: Cascaded Mixture-of-Experts Learning for Near-Shortest Path Routing
summary: While deep learning models that leverage local features have demonstrated significant potential for near-optimal routing in dense Euclidean graphs, they struggle to generalize well in sparse networks where topological irregularities require broader structural awareness. To address this limitation, we train a Cascaded Mixture of Experts (Ca-MoE) to solve the all-pairs near-shortest path (APNSP) routing problem. Our Ca-MoE is a modular two-tier architecture that supports the decision-making for forwarder selection with lower-tier experts relying on local features and upper-tier experts relying on global features. It performs adaptive inference wherein the upper-tier experts are triggered only when the lower-tier ones do not suffice to achieve adequate decision quality. Computational efficiency is thus achieved by escalating model capacity only when necessitated by topological complexity, and parameter redundancy is avoided. Furthermore, we incorporate an online meta-learning strategy that facilitates independent expert fine-tuning and utilizes a stability-focused update mechanism to prevent catastrophic forgetting as new graph environments are encountered. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that Ca-MoE routing improves accuracy by up to 29.1% in sparse networks compared to single-expert baselines and maintains performance within 1%-6% of the theoretical upper bound across diverse graph densities.

AI.tldr: A two-tier cascaded mixture of experts (Ca-MoE) architecture for adaptive routing in sparse networks, using local features first and escalating to global features only when needed, with online meta-learning to prevent catastrophic forgetting.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models using local features work well for dense Euclidean graphs but struggle to generalize in sparse networks with topological irregularities that require broader structural awareness.
AI.method: Two-tier Ca-MoE architecture with lower-tier experts using local features and upper-tier experts using global features, performing adaptive inference where upper-tier is only triggered when lower-tier is insufficient. Incorporates online meta-learning for independent expert fine-tuning with stability-focused updates to prevent catastrophic forgetting.
AI.result: Ca-MoE routing improves accuracy by up to 29.1% in sparse networks compared to single-expert baselines and maintains performance within 1%-6% of theoretical upper bound across diverse graph densities.
AI.conclusion: The Ca-MoE architecture effectively addresses the generalization challenges in sparse networks by adaptively combining local and global features, achieving near-optimal routing performance while maintaining computational efficiency through selective expert activation.

title: Predictive Uncertainty in Short-Term PV Forecasting under Missing Data: A Multiple Imputation Approach
summary: Missing values are common in photovoltaic (PV) power data, yet the uncertainty they induce is not propagated into predictive distributions. We develop a framework that incorporates missing-data uncertainty into short-term PV forecasting by combining stochastic multiple imputation with Rubin's rule. The approach is model-agnostic and can be integrated with standard machine-learning predictors. Empirical results show that ignoring missing-data uncertainty leads to overly narrow prediction intervals. Accounting for this uncertainty improves interval calibration while maintaining comparable point prediction accuracy. These results demonstrate the importance of propagating imputation uncertainty in data-driven PV forecasting.

AI.tldr: A framework for PV forecasting that incorporates missing-data uncertainty through stochastic imputation and Rubin's rule, improving prediction interval calibration without sacrificing point accuracy.
AI.motivation: Missing values are common in PV power data, but the uncertainty from these missing values is not currently propagated into predictive distributions, potentially leading to overconfident predictions.
AI.method: Develops a model-agnostic framework combining stochastic multiple imputation with Rubin's rule to incorporate missing-data uncertainty into short-term PV forecasting, compatible with standard machine-learning predictors.
AI.result: Empirical results show that ignoring missing-data uncertainty leads to overly narrow prediction intervals, while accounting for this uncertainty improves interval calibration while maintaining comparable point prediction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The research demonstrates the importance of propagating imputation uncertainty in data-driven PV forecasting to obtain more reliable prediction intervals.

title: Mamba-3: Improved Sequence Modeling using State Space Principles
summary: Scaling inference-time compute has emerged as an important driver of LLM performance, making inference efficiency a central focus of model design alongside model quality. While the current Transformer-based models deliver strong model quality, their quadratic compute and linear memory make inference expensive. This has spurred the development of sub-quadratic models with reduced linear compute and constant memory requirements. However, many recent linear models trade off model quality and capability for algorithmic efficiency, failing on tasks such as state tracking. Moreover, their theoretically linear inference remains hardware-inefficient in practice. Guided by an inference-first perspective, we introduce three core methodological improvements inspired by the state space model (SSM) viewpoint of linear models. We combine: (1) a more expressive recurrence derived from SSM discretization, (2) a complex-valued state update rule that enables richer state tracking, and (3) a multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) formulation for better model performance without increasing decode latency. Together with architectural refinements, our Mamba-3 model achieves significant gains across retrieval, state-tracking, and downstream language modeling tasks. At the 1.5B scale, Mamba-3 improves average downstream accuracy by 0.6 percentage points compared to the next best model (Gated DeltaNet), with Mamba-3's MIMO variant further improving accuracy by another 1.2 points for a total 1.8 point gain. Across state-size experiments, Mamba-3 achieves comparable perplexity to Mamba-2 despite using half of its predecessor's state size. Our evaluations demonstrate Mamba-3's ability to advance the performance-efficiency Pareto frontier.

AI.tldr: Mamba-3 introduces SSM-inspired improvements (expressive recurrence, complex-valued state updates, MIMO formulation) to create a sub-quadratic model that significantly outperforms alternatives on language tasks while maintaining efficient inference.
AI.motivation: Inference efficiency has become a critical factor in LLM performance, but current Transformer models have quadratic compute and linear memory costs. Recent sub-quadratic models often sacrifice model quality and capability (especially on tasks like state tracking) for algorithmic efficiency, and their theoretically linear inference remains hardware-inefficient in practice.
AI.method: Three core methodological improvements inspired by the state space model (SSM) viewpoint: 1) a more expressive recurrence from SSM discretization, 2) a complex-valued state update rule enabling richer state tracking, and 3) a multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) formulation for better performance without increasing decode latency, combined with architectural refinements.
AI.result: Mamba-3 achieves significant gains across retrieval, state-tracking, and downstream language modeling tasks. At 1.5B scale, Mamba-3 improves average downstream accuracy by 0.6 percentage points over the next best model (Gated DeltaNet), with the MIMO variant adding another 1.2 points for a total 1.8 point gain. Mamba-3 achieves comparable perplexity to Mamba-2 while using half the state size.
AI.conclusion: Mamba-3 advances the performance-efficiency Pareto frontier by delivering superior model quality while maintaining efficient inference, addressing the trade-offs that have plagued previous sub-quadratic models.

title: Unbiased and Biased Variance-Reduced Forward-Reflected-Backward Splitting Methods for Stochastic Composite Inclusions
summary: This paper develops new variance-reduction techniques for the forward-reflected-backward splitting (FRBS) method to solve a class of possibly nonmonotone stochastic composite inclusions. Unlike unbiased estimators such as mini-batching, developing stochastic biased variants faces a fundamental technical challenge and has not been utilized before for inclusions and fixed-point problems. We fill this gap by designing a new framework that can handle both unbiased and biased estimators. Our main idea is to construct stochastic variance-reduced estimators for the forward-reflected direction and use them to perform iterate updates. First, we propose a class of unbiased variance-reduced estimators and show that increasing mini-batch SGD, loopless-SVRG, and SAGA estimators fall within this class. For these unbiased estimators, we establish a $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ best-iterate convergence rate for the expected squared residual norm, together with almost-sure convergence of the iterate sequence to a solution. Consequently, we prove that the best oracle complexities for the $n$-finite-sum and expectation settings are $\mathcal{O}(n^{2/3}ε^{-2})$ and $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-10/3})$, respectively, when employing loopless-SVRG or SAGA, where $ε$ is a desired accuracy. Second, we introduce a new class of biased variance-reduced estimators for the forward-reflected direction, which includes SARAH, Hybrid SGD, and Hybrid SVRG as special instances. While the convergence rates remain valid for these biased estimators, the resulting oracle complexities are $\mathcal{O}(n^{3/4}ε^{-2})$ and $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-5})$ for the $n$-finite-sum and expectation settings, respectively. Finally, we conduct two numerical experiments on AUC optimization for imbalanced classification and policy evaluation in reinforcement learning.

AI.tldr: The paper develops new variance-reduction techniques for the forward-reflected-backward splitting method to solve stochastic composite inclusions, introducing both unbiased and biased estimators to improve convergence rates and oracle complexities.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental technical challenge in developing stochastic biased variants for inclusions and fixed-point problems, unlike unbiased estimators like mini-batching. The paper aims to fill this gap by designing a framework that can handle both unbiased and biased estimators for variance reduction.
AI.method: The authors construct stochastic variance-reduced estimators for the forward-reflected direction and use them for iterate updates. They propose: 1) a class of unbiased variance-reduced estimators (including increasing mini-batch SGD, loopless-SVRG, and SAGA), and 2) a new class of biased variance-reduced estimators (including SARAH, Hybrid SGD, and Hybrid SVRG).
AI.result: For unbiased estimators: established O(1/k) best-iterate convergence rate for expected squared residual norm, with almost-sure convergence. Best oracle complexities: O(n^{2/3}ε^{-2}) for n-finite-sum and O(ε^{-10/3}) for expectation settings. For biased estimators: convergence rates remain valid but with oracle complexities O(n^{3/4}ε^{-2}) and O(ε^{-5}) respectively. Numerical experiments on AUC optimization and reinforcement learning policy evaluation demonstrate effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully develops a new framework for variance reduction in FRBS methods that handles both unbiased and biased estimators, providing improved convergence guarantees and oracle complexities for stochastic composite inclusion problems, with practical applications in machine learning tasks.

title: Effective Distillation to Hybrid xLSTM Architectures
summary: There have been numerous attempts to distill quadratic attention-based large language models (LLMs) into sub-quadratic linearized architectures. However, despite extensive research, such distilled models often fail to match the performance of their teacher LLMs on various downstream tasks. We set out the goal of lossless distillation, which we define in terms of tolerance-corrected Win-and-Tie rates between student and teacher on sets of tasks. To this end, we introduce an effective distillation pipeline for xLSTM-based students. We propose an additional merging stage, where individually linearized experts are combined into a single model. We show the effectiveness of this pipeline by distilling base and instruction-tuned models from the Llama, Qwen, and Olmo families. In many settings, our xLSTM-based students recover most of the teacher's performance, and even exceed it on some downstream tasks. Our contributions are an important step towards more energy-efficient and cost-effective replacements for transformer-based LLMs.

AI.tldr: Distilling quadratic attention-based LLMs into sub-quadratic linearized xLSTM architectures with a merging stage achieves near-lossless performance, often matching or exceeding teacher models on downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Previous attempts at distilling large language models from quadratic attention-based architectures to sub-quadratic linearized ones have consistently fallen short of matching teacher performance on downstream tasks, highlighting the need for more effective distillation methods.
AI.method: The authors propose a novel distillation pipeline for xLSTM-based students that includes an additional merging stage where individually linearized experts are combined into a single model. They evaluate this approach by distilling base and instruction-tuned models from Llama, Qwen, and Olmo families, using tolerance-corrected Win-and-Tie rates to define lossless distillation.
AI.result: The xLSTM-based students recover most of the teacher's performance and even exceed it on some downstream tasks, demonstrating effective knowledge transfer and improved efficiency compared to transformer-based LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed distillation pipeline with expert merging represents an important step toward more energy-efficient and cost-effective replacements for transformer-based LLMs, achieving near-lossless performance transfer from quadratic to sub-quadratic architectures.

title: Robust and Computationally Efficient Linear Contextual Bandits under Adversarial Corruption and Heavy-Tailed Noise
summary: We study linear contextual bandits under adversarial corruption and heavy-tailed noise with finite $(1+ε)$-th moments for some $ε\in (0,1]$. Existing work that addresses both adversarial corruption and heavy-tailed noise relies on a finite variance (i.e., finite second-moment) assumption and suffers from computational inefficiency. We propose a computationally efficient algorithm based on online mirror descent that achieves robustness to both adversarial corruption and heavy-tailed noise. While the existing algorithm incurs $\mathcal{O}(t\log T)$ computational cost, our algorithm reduces this to $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per round. We establish an additive regret bound consisting of a term depending on the $(1+ε)$-moment bound of the noise and a term depending on the total amount of corruption. In particular, when $ε= 1$, our result recovers existing guarantees under finite-variance assumptions. When no corruption is present, it matches the best-known rates for linear contextual bandits with heavy-tailed noise. Moreover, the algorithm requires no prior knowledge of the noise moment bound or the total amount of corruption and still guarantees sublinear regret.

AI.tldr: Computationally efficient algorithm for linear contextual bandits under adversarial corruption and heavy-tailed noise, reducing per-round cost from O(t log T) to O(1) and achieving robust regret bounds without prior knowledge of noise or corruption.
AI.motivation: Existing algorithms for linear contextual bandits under adversarial corruption and heavy-tailed noise rely on finite variance assumptions and suffer from high computational cost (O(t log T) per round). There is a need for a computationally efficient algorithm that can handle both adversarial corruption and heavy-tailed noise with only finite (1+ε)-th moment assumptions.
AI.method: The algorithm is based on online mirror descent, designed to be robust to both adversarial corruption and heavy-tailed noise with finite (1+ε)-th moments.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm achieves O(1) computational cost per round, significantly improving over the existing O(t log T) cost. It establishes an additive regret bound that depends on the (1+ε)-moment bound of the noise and the total corruption amount. When ε=1, it recovers finite-variance guarantees, and with no corruption, it matches best-known rates for heavy-tailed noise. The algorithm requires no prior knowledge of noise moment bounds or corruption amount.
AI.conclusion: The proposed online mirror descent-based algorithm provides a computationally efficient solution for linear contextual bandits under adversarial corruption and heavy-tailed noise, achieving improved computational complexity and robust performance without requiring prior knowledge of noise or corruption parameters.

title: SmartSearch: How Ranking Beats Structure for Conversational Memory Retrieval
summary: Recent conversational memory systems invest heavily in LLM-based structuring at ingestion time and learned retrieval policies at query time. We show that neither is necessary. SmartSearch retrieves from raw, unstructured conversation history using a fully deterministic pipeline: NER-weighted substring matching for recall, rule-based entity discovery for multi-hop expansion, and a CrossEncoder+ColBERT rank fusion stage -- the only learned component -- running on CPU in ~650ms. Oracle analysis on two benchmarks identifies a compilation bottleneck: retrieval recall reaches 98.6%, but without intelligent ranking only 22.5% of gold evidence survives truncation to the token budget. With score-adaptive truncation and no per-dataset tuning, SmartSearch achieves 93.5% on LoCoMo and 88.4% on LongMemEval-S, exceeding all known memory systems under the same evaluation protocol on both benchmarks while using 8.5x fewer tokens than full-context baselines.

AI.tldr: SmartSearch introduces a deterministic retrieval pipeline for conversational memory that achieves state-of-the-art performance without complex LLM structuring or learned retrieval policies, using only one learned component for ranking.
AI.motivation: Current conversational memory systems rely heavily on LLM-based structuring during ingestion and learned retrieval policies at query time, which are complex and resource-intensive. The authors aim to show that neither is necessary for effective retrieval.
AI.method: SmartSearch uses a fully deterministic pipeline: NER-weighted substring matching for recall, rule-based entity discovery for multi-hop expansion, and a CrossEncoder+ColBERT rank fusion stage (the only learned component) that runs on CPU in ~650ms. It also employs score-adaptive truncation to fit within token budgets.
AI.result: SmartSearch achieves 93.5% on LoCoMo and 88.4% on LongMemEval-S, exceeding all known memory systems under the same evaluation protocol on both benchmarks while using 8.5x fewer tokens than full-context baselines. Retrieval recall reaches 98.6% but without intelligent ranking only 22.5% of gold evidence survives truncation.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that effective conversational memory retrieval can be achieved with a simple, deterministic pipeline that avoids complex LLM-based structuring and learned retrieval policies, while significantly reducing computational costs and maintaining high performance.

title: HorizonMath: Measuring AI Progress Toward Mathematical Discovery with Automatic Verification
summary: Can AI make progress on important, unsolved mathematical problems? Large language models are now capable of sophisticated mathematical and scientific reasoning, but whether they can perform novel research is still widely debated and underexplored. We introduce HorizonMath, a benchmark of over 100 predominantly unsolved problems spanning 8 domains in computational and applied mathematics, paired with an open-source evaluation framework for automated verification. Our benchmark targets a class of problems where discovery is hard, requiring meaningful mathematical insight, but verification is computationally efficient and simple. Because these solutions are unknown, HorizonMath is immune to data contamination, and most state-of-the-art models score near 0%. Existing research-level benchmarks instead rely on formal proof verification or manual review, both of which are expensive to scale. Using this platform, we find two problems for which GPT 5.4 Pro proposes solutions that improve on the best-known published results, representing potential novel contributions (pending expert review). We release HorizonMath as an open challenge and a growing community resource, where correct solutions to problems in the unsolved problem classes could constitute novel results in the mathematical literature.

AI.tldr: HorizonMath is a benchmark of unsolved mathematical problems designed to test whether AI can perform novel research, with GPT-5.4 Pro proposing solutions that improve on best-known results.
AI.motivation: To determine if AI can perform novel mathematical research and solve important unsolved problems, while addressing limitations of existing benchmarks (expensive verification methods and data contamination).
AI.method: Created HorizonMath benchmark with over 100 unsolved problems spanning 8 domains, focusing on problems where discovery is hard but verification is computationally efficient and simple. Used an open-source evaluation framework for automated verification.
AI.result: GPT-5.4 Pro proposed solutions that improve on best-known published results for two problems, suggesting potential novel contributions. Most state-of-the-art models score near 0% due to benchmark immunity to data contamination.
AI.conclusion: HorizonMath serves as an effective platform for testing AI's ability to perform novel mathematical research, potentially leading to real mathematical contributions, and offers a scalable alternative to existing expensive verification methods.
